target.title: fix(heartbeat): suppress fallback after route-matched message tool
target.body:
When heartbeat runs in message-tool-only mode, a successful generic message tool delivery can still be followed by duplicate free-text fallback narration in the same Telegram chat.

Fixes #84217

## Affected surface

- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`
  - Adds route-matched message tool delivery evidence for heartbeat replies only after the generic message tool sent to the same provider/target/account route.
- `src/auto-reply/reply-payload.ts`
  - Adds internal reply-payload metadata for route-scoped message tool delivery evidence.
- `src/auto-reply/heartbeat-reply-payload.ts`
  - Adds a heartbeat-specific evidence reader.
- `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts`
  - Suppresses free-text fallback only when the reply carries route-scoped message tool delivery evidence.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.ts`
  - Leaves heartbeat payloads available for heartbeat-specific route evidence instead of dropping them via generic message-tool dedupe first.
- Tests:
  - `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.tool-response.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts`

## Scope

This is a parameter/evidence threading fix. It does not add a config flag, does not change heartbeat scheduling, and does not touch Slack identity, commitment, or channel-specific private paths.

The important semantic boundary is that "generic message tool delivery evidence" is not the same as "has outbound reply content". This PR only marks heartbeat replies when existing message-tool target evidence matches the same delivery route via the existing `resolveMessagingToolPayloadDedupe` route matcher. Global `messagingToolSentTexts` without route targets is intentionally not enough.

After Vincent's thread-route follow-up, I found one extra current-head issue while refreshing proof: generic reply-payload dedupe could drop the matched heartbeat payload before `heartbeat-runner` saw the route evidence. The current head keeps heartbeat payloads out of that generic dedupe layer so the heartbeat-specific suppression path receives the evidence and can still preserve no-route / wrong-topic fallback behavior.

## Prior art

Prior attempt #84273 was closed by contributor due to inability to provide live channel proof. This PR provides expanded source + runtime helper proof, with the helper semantic explicitly distinguishing "generic message tool delivery evidence" from "has outbound reply content" to address the message-delivery risk flagged in #84273.

The key difference from #84273 is route scope: #84273 marked heartbeat payloads after any message-tool send, while this PR only marks a heartbeat payload when committed `messagingToolSentTargets` match the reply route. If the message tool delivered to another route, or only global text evidence exists, heartbeat fallback still sends.

## Current-head P1 follow-up proof

This update addresses the ClawSweeper P1 finding "Suppress same-route fallback even when the text differs" on head `25958135ed2135dede29e716668ac34cfaea8007`.

The same-route heartbeat evidence rule now treats committed route-matched generic `message` tool target evidence as sufficient for a single plain heartbeat fallback payload, even when the fallback text differs from the tool body. Multi-payload heartbeat replies keep the existing text-match guard so unrelated fallback payloads are not suppressed. Rich payloads, media payloads, reasoning, no-route evidence, wrong-route evidence, and wrong-thread/topic evidence remain outside this suppression path.

Commands run locally:

```text
$ pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts -t "same-route message-tool delivery used different text"
RED before implementation:
AssertionError: expected undefined to be true

$ pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts -t "same-route message-tool delivery used different text"
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 46 skipped (47)

$ pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.tool-response.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  17 passed (17)
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  47 passed (47)

$ pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts -t "heartbeat replies"
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  9 passed | 55 skipped (64)

$ pnpm check:changed --base origin/main
exit 0

$ git diff --check origin/main..HEAD
exit 0

$ gitleaks protect --staged --redact
INF no leaks found
```

### Updated live Telegram proof for head `25958135ed2135dede29e716668ac34cfaea8007`

The earlier Mantis Telegram workflow could not run because this actor lacks write/maintain/admin permission, so I reran the current-head proof locally with the existing Telegram proof credential and a temporary harness outside the patch. The harness used the real current-head route-evidence path:

- `buildReplyPayloads(...)` built heartbeat payloads from route-scoped `messagingToolSentTargets`.
- `runHeartbeatOnce(...)` made the heartbeat delivery/suppression decision.

## Current-head maintenance proof

After Vincent's route/thread follow-up moved this PR to `f21343bfd4`, CI exposed one current-head regression: `checks-node-auto-reply-reply-agent-runner` timed out in `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts` at `dedupes duplicate same-target reply text without suppressing unrelated finals`.

Root cause: route dedupe called `getChannelPlugin("telegram")` from the generic reply-payload path, which lazily loads the bundled Telegram plugin in this test/runtime path and can block the shard. Current head `df6f33925b` keeps the route/thread semantics from Vincent's follow-up, uses only the already-loaded channel registry for plugin-specific matching, and preserves internal route-string fallback for forms like `telegram:group:<chat>:topic:<thread>` without loading bundled channel implementations.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** heartbeat message-tool-only delivery should not send duplicate free-text fallback after the generic `message` tool has already delivered to the same route, but it must still send fallback when no same-route delivery evidence exists.
- **Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, Node 22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.0, branch `wolf/heartbeat-suppress-after-message-tool`, head `14b8854108`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran a local OpenClaw runtime helper that imports production `runHeartbeatOnce`, seeds a real `openclaw.json`-shaped config and session store, registers a local Telegram outbound adapter, then executes three heartbeat ticks: same-route evidence, no-route evidence, and wrong-thread/topic-unmarked evidence.
- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the runtime helper:

- **Observed result after fix:** with route-scoped message-tool evidence, `runHeartbeatOnce` completes and does not invoke the Telegram send path (`telegramSendCount: 0`), so the fallback narration is suppressed. Without route evidence, and for the wrong-topic/wrong-thread-unmarked fallback case, the same runtime path invokes the Telegram send path once (`telegramSendCount: 1`), proving fallback remains available when the message tool did not prove same-route delivery.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run a live Telegram heartbeat tick with a real bot token/chat. I did not test Slack/WhatsApp live channels; the patch is heartbeat reply evidence and generic runner fallback suppression, with Telegram-shaped runtime coverage for the reported duplicate free-text path.

target.title: fix(cron): allow timeoutSeconds: 0 for no-timeout mode
target.body:
## 🐛 Problem

The cron job UI validation logic incorrectly rejects `timeoutSeconds: 0`, which should represent "no timeout" mode.

**Current behavior:**
```typescript
if (timeout <= 0) {
  errors.timeoutSeconds = "cron.errors.timeoutInvalid";
}
```
This prevents users from setting `timeoutSeconds = 0` to disable timeout entirely.

## ✅ Solution

Update validation to allow `0` as a valid value (meaning "no timeout"):

```typescript
// 0 = no timeout, > 0 = timeout in seconds
if (timeout !== 0 && timeout <= 0) {
  errors.timeoutSeconds = "cron.errors.timeoutInvalid";
}
```

## 📁 Files Changed

- `ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.ts` - Updated `validateCronForm` function

## 🧪 Testing

- Create a cron job with `timeoutSeconds: 0`
- Verify it saves without validation errors
- Confirm the job runs without timeout restrictions

## 📊 Impact

- **User-facing**: Users can now disable timeout for long-running cron jobs
- **Backward compatible**: Existing jobs with `timeoutSeconds > 0` are unaffected
- **Risk**: Low - single line change, clear semantic intent

## 📝 Related

Closes #41272

target.title: feat(android): add Wear OS app MVP
target.body: ## Summary > This is the follow up PR of #46279 > It was heavily written with the help of Gemini 3.1 Pro (mainly for building UI) and Codex GPT 5.4, including lots of review iterations. > During the implementation of the previous PR I moved more and more logic into a shared layer, which caused lots of review comments about code I didn't write. I tried to let Codex write regression tests for several of these cases to ensure that I don't break features. > Manual testing with my Pixel Watch 2 and Pixel 8 Pro was part of the whole process. Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: OpenClaw did not have a Wear OS companion. - Why it matters: The watch needs a compact, reliable chat surface with fast reply actions, honest connection state, and behavior that still works on tiny round screens and when the app is backgrounded. - What changed: Added a new Wear OS app module, phone-side Wear proxy service for Tailscale support, direct watch gateway client, transcript-first chat UI, session picker, split settings screens, tile + complication support, background reply notifications with quick reply. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change gateway orchestration semantics, tools/skills behavior, desktop/iOS/macOS clients, or the Android phone app's broader UI theme beyond the phone-side Wear proxy integration. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # (none) - Related # (none) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - New Wear OS companion app with a transcript-first chat flow and a two-page swipe layout. - Chat opens on the transcript page and follows the latest message by default; the primary edge action switches between `Reply` and `Latest`. - Added session picker, chat settings, connection settings, reconnect flow, and direct-vs-phone-proxy connection support on the watch. - Added Wear tile and complication entry points. - Added native watch notifications for background replies, including quick reply via text/voice dictation. - Added optional TTS playback and configurable screen-awake behavior on the watch. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes`) - New/changed network calls? (`Yes`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`Yes`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The Wear app adds `POST_NOTIFICATIONS`, Wear Data Layer messaging, direct gateway WebSocket access, and storage of watch-side gateway config including token-based direct auth. Risk is constrained by using the existing authenticated gateway paths, limiting watch data access to the same session/gateway calls already exposed by the phone client, honoring platform notification permission, and keeping watch auth/config local to the Wear app. Phone-proxy traffic stays within the paired phone/watch channel and only proxies requests through the phone's active operator session. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Android phone + Wear OS 3/4 watches; manually tested on Pixel Watch 2 and Wear round emulators (small + large) - Runtime/container: Android Studio/Gradle debug builds - Model/provider: Existing OpenClaw gateway via phone proxy and direct gateway modes - Integration/channel (if any): Wear Data Layer between paired phone and watch - Relevant config (redacted): Matching debug-signed phone + watch builds; direct mode host/port/token/TLS when used; phone-proxy mode with phone app installed and signed the same way ### Steps 1. Build and install matching debug phone and Wear builds, then launch the Wear app on a paired watch. 2. Verify transcript flow, reply actions, session switching, settings, direct mode, phone-proxy mode, tile/complication entry points, and reopen/background behavior. 3. Send assistant replies while the watch app is foregrounded and backgrounded, then verify reply notifications, quick reply, reconnect/disconnect behavior, and latest-message scrolling. ### Expected - The watch should show a compact, reliable chat UX with correct connection state, preserved transcript state, working reply paths, and background reply notifications. ### Actual - Matches expected in manual testing on Pixel Watch 2 and both small/large Wear emulators. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Actual screenshots from Pixel Watch 2: | | | | |---|---|---| | Empty tanscript view | Empty transcript view | Swipe over to menu | | Scroll down in menu | Scroll further down | Click behavior settings button | | Scroll down | Click screen awake setting | Click screen awake setting again | | Go back and enter connection settings | Scroll down to helper text | Disable phone proxy | | Scroll down to gateway config | Scroll further down | Go back to transcript view | | Scroll down to see reply edge button (*default view* if you open the app≈) | Reply button toggles voice input (or let's you pick first, if disabled in settings) | Thinking animation | | When closing app, notification is delivered| Tile adding dialog with preview of tile | Actual tile | | Watch face with complication at top and bottom right (different formats) | ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Manually tested on a Pixel Watch 2 via Tailscale phone proxy and in both small and large round Wear emulators. Verified chat launch, latest-message behavior, reply flow, session picker, chat settings, connection settings, direct gateway mode, phone-proxy mode, tile launch, complication launch, background reply notifications, and quick reply from the notification. - Edge cases checked: Reopening the app after backgrounding, disconnect/reconnect flow, switching away from and back to the transcript page, scrolling away from latest and jumping back, and testing on both small and large round watch layouts. - What you did **not** verify: every possible Android OEM pairing/background-policy variant, and exhaustive real-network failure cases beyond the implemented reconnect/error handling paths. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`No`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: None. ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the Wear branch commits or ship only the phone build without installing the Wear module; on-device, switch the watch back to phone-proxy or disconnect direct mode if direct gateway behavior is problematic. - Files/config to restore: `apps/android/wear/**`, `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/wear/WearProxyService.kt`, `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/NodeRuntime.kt`, `apps/android/app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml`, and `apps/android/app/build.gradle.kts`. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: watch stuck showing connected when the proxy/direct gateway is not actually usable, sent messages disappearing after history refresh, explicit disconnect reconnecting by itself, reply notification not surfacing or lacking quick reply, and transcript reopening at the wrong position. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Phone-proxy mode depends on both Wear Data Layer reachability and the phone app having a live operator session. - Mitigation: The watch exposes direct mode as an alternative, surfaces connection state in the UI, and includes reconnect/disconnect controls. - Risk: The second page is dense on the smallest round watches, especially with long session names/keys. - Mitigation: Manual testing covered both small and large round emulator sizes and a real Pixel Watch 2. - Risk: Background reply notifications depend on platform permission and Wear notification presentation. - Mitigation: The app requests notification permission explicitly, and manual verification covered notification delivery plus quick reply behavior.

target.title: refactor: add transcript reader seam
target.body:
## What

Path 3 / PR 3.1b transcript readers: add a storage-agnostic transcript reader facade and route storage-neutral transcript read/tail/stats/message lookup/projection callers through it. Refs #88838.

## Why

The session accessor seam from PR 3.1a gives the SQLite decomposition a clean branch-by-abstraction point, but transcript readers still needed a caller-facing seam before any SQLite-backed transcript store can replace the file-backed implementation. This keeps current file-backed behavior while removing direct reader coupling from gateway, agent, status, and TUI caller paths.

## Changes

- Add `src/gateway/session-transcript-readers.ts`
- Route gateway transcript readers
- Route agent/TUI/status readers
- Update reader mocks and tests
- Keep `session-utils.ts` compatibility exports
- Leave auto-reply/file-semantic paths unchanged

| Area | Change |
|------|--------|
| Gateway chat/history | Scoped reads for chat history, message lookup, SSE history, artifacts, image attachment indexing, reset hooks, and session events |
| Agent tools/runtime | Scoped reads for embedded gateway stub, sessions history/list tools, subagent output/recovery, and restart recovery |
| Compatibility | `session-utils.ts` reader re-exports now pass through the transcript reader facade |

Source branch commits:

- `cdeb1b4cc5` - `refactor: add transcript reader seam facade`
- `be70ff961e` - `refactor: route transcript readers through facade`

No storage flip, runtime dual-read/fallback, SQLite schema work, or SQLite runtime wiring is included. No current blockers.

## Summary

- Adds a dedicated SQLite state benchmark harness that seeds real OpenClaw shared and per-agent SQLite databases, runs integrity checks, measures hot queries, checkpoints WAL files, writes JSON artifacts, and prints stable `SQLITE_PERF_*` proof lines.
- Adds package scripts for smoke, default, and large SQLite perf runs.
- Adds query-plan regression coverage for shared state and per-agent cache hot paths so index drift fails in Vitest instead of relying on benchmark timing.
- Intentionally out of scope: broader SQLite resilience, backup/restore inventory, and unrelated gateway E2E failures from the SQLite plan execution.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #91616

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #

Maintainer-requested local BUG-005 extraction from the SQLite validation plan.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Real environment tested: Local Linux source checkout on branch `bug-005-sqlite-perf-query-plan`, Node `v24.15.0` runtime available in the workspace, with repo dependencies resolved through the existing checkout install.

- What was not tested: The default and large SQLite perf profiles were not run locally; no full `pnpm check`, full `pnpm test`, Docker, cross-OS, or live provider lane was run.

target.title: Fix stuck-session recovery aborts for active reply runs
target.body:
﻿## Summary
- preserve `stuck_recovery` through reply-run session-id fallback and embedded-run cancel plumbing so recovery no longer degrades into a fake user abort
- require stale tracked progress before active embedded/reply work is abort-drained, even when active-abort recovery is enabled
- add regressions for fresh terminal progress, fresh active reply work, and streamed model progress so long-but-active runs stay alive

## Testing
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.logging.config.ts src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.integration.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-embedded-agent.config.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Verify that this patch preserves `stuck_recovery` through the reply-run session-id fallback and does not abort a live embedded run that still has fresh tracked progress.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows 11 using the real modules from this PR branch via a Node/tsx runtime harness after commit `159943a6c0`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran `pnpm exec tsx -` against the real repo modules in this branch with a runtime harness that (1) creates a reply operation and aborts it through `abortReplyRunBySessionId(..., { reason: "stuck_recovery" })`, then (2) registers an active embedded run, records fresh progress with `markDiagnosticRunProgressForTest(...)`, and calls `recoverStuckDiagnosticSession(...)` with `allowActiveAbort: true`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the local OpenClaw runtime harness after the fix:
  [reply-registry]
  {"aborted":true,"result":{"kind":"aborted","code":"aborted_by_system","reason":"stuck_recovery"},"abortSignalReason":"Reply operation aborted by system reason=stuck_recovery"}
  [stuck-recovery]
  {"snapshotBefore":{"activeWorkKind":"embedded_run","hasActiveEmbeddedRun":true,"lastProgressAgeMs":1,"lastProgressReason":"model chunk observed from runtime harness"},"outcome":{"status":"skipped","action":"observe_only","reason":"active_embedded_run","sessionId":"session-live-progress","sessionKey":"agent:main:main","activeSessionId":"session-live-progress","activeWorkKind":"embedded_run"},"abortCount":0,"cancelReasons":[],"snapshotAfter":{"activeWorkKind":"embedded_run","hasActiveEmbeddedRun":true,"lastProgressAgeMs":84,"lastProgressReason":"model chunk observed from runtime harness"}}
  [diagnostic] stuck session recovery skipped: sessionId=session-live-progress sessionKey=agent:main:main age=180s queueDepth=1 activeSessionId=session-live-progress
  [diagnostic] stuck session recovery outcome: status=skipped action=observe_only sessionId=session-live-progress sessionKey=agent:main:main activeSessionId=session-live-progress activeWorkKind=embedded_run reason=active_embedded_run
- **Observed result after fix**: The reply-run fallback keeps `reason: "stuck_recovery"` instead of degrading into a user-abort shape. The live-progress recovery path returns `status: "skipped"`, leaves the embedded run active, and does not call abort or cancel on the active handle.
- **What was not tested**: I did not rerun a multi-minute end-to-end Feishu + `claude-cli` channel repro in this environment, and I did not capture UI/video proof from a live chat surface.

## Notes
- `diagnostics.stuckSessionWarnMs` / `diagnostics.stuckSessionAbortMs` and the default `warnMs x 3` / minimum 5 minute behavior are already documented in the current docs/config help, so this PR focuses on the runtime regressions from `#88870`.

Closes #88870

target.title: fix(memory): do not prune session index from a failed directory scan
target.body:
## Summary

A full memory sync treats source enumerations as the source of truth for **destructive** maintenance: stale-row pruning of the session and memory-file indexes (`files` + `chunks` + vectors + FTS), full reindex rebuilds, and exported-session reconciliation. These enumerations swallowed filesystem errors and returned an empty listing, so a single transient scan failure (NFS `EIO`/`ESTALE`, permission blip) was indistinguishable from an empty directory and wiped the corresponding index — followed by an expensive full re-read/re-embed after the next successful scan. The failed-scan prune test in this PR fails on current `main` (the indexed row gets deleted), confirming the hazard is live.

### Sessions (original scope)

- `packages/memory-host-sdk`: add `scanSessionFilesForAgent(agentId): { ok, files }`. `ok: true` means the directory was actually read or authoritatively does not exist (see below); `ok: false` means the scan failed. `listSessionFilesForAgent` delegates to it and is unchanged for its non-destructive callers.
- `resolveMemorySessionSyncPlan` takes a required `scanOk` and returns `pruneStaleRows`, true only for a full enumeration that authoritatively read the directory.
- `syncSessionFiles` gates the stale-row prune on `pruneStaleRows`. A failed scan skips the prune (with a warning) and leaves the index intact for the next successful enumeration.

### Follow-up hardening (from review)

- A missing sessions dir is authoritative-empty only when the parent agent dir exists: fresh agents (the dir appears on first transcript write) prune orphans without log noise, while an unreachable state tree (e.g. unmounted volume) counts as a failed scan.
- `MemorySourceSyncPlan` carries `scanOk`; `runSync` no longer clears `dirty`/`sessionsDirty`/`sessionsDirtyFiles` after a failed scan, so the next eligible sync retries instead of reporting a clean sync that indexed nothing.
- The memory-file sibling gets the same guard: new `scanMemoryFiles(): { ok, files }` (exported via `engine-storage` and the plugin SDK barrel) with explicit root `readdir` probes — fs-safe `walkDirectory` swallows readdir errors even at the walk root, so an lstat-only check would still report `ok` on the exact failure class this PR targets. `syncMemoryFiles` skips its stale-row prune on a failed enumeration.
- Full reindexes abort (`assertReindexScanOk`) instead of swapping in an empty index built from a failed scan; the safe path restores the original DB on throw, the unsafe path skips `writeMeta` so the next sync retries the rebuild.
- `exportSessions` skips its reconcile-delete pass on a failed listing so one blip cannot delete the exported session-markdown corpus.
- Renamed the local CLI diagnostics in `cli.runtime.ts` (`scanMemoryFiles` → `diagnoseMemoryFiles`, `scanSessionFiles` → `diagnoseSessionFiles`) to avoid colliding with the new SDK export surfaced through the same barrel.
- Fixed a stale assertion in `manager-sync-ops.startup-catchup.test.ts` that fails on `main` today: it still expects the pre-#89138 `void` return of `syncSessionFiles`.

### Why skip on *failed*, not on *empty*

Deletions/resets archive to usage-counted `.deleted.`/`.reset.` artifacts that still appear in the listing, and `disk-budget` can physically remove the last archive — so an **authoritatively empty** directory with a non-empty index is legitimately reachable. In that case we must still prune, or deleted-session content would linger in search forever. Skipping only on a *failed* scan prevents the wipe without introducing persistent orphan rows.

### Known residual gaps (follow-ups)

- Subtree readdir failures below the probed roots remain invisible (`@openclaw/fs-safe` `walkDirectory` reports no errors); blast radius is the affected subtree, not the whole index. A clean fix needs an additive fs-safe API (e.g. `WalkDirectoryResult.failedDirCount`).
- `resolveCanonicalRootMemoryFile` swallows workspace-root readdir errors; bounded to the single root memory file.

## Verification

Worktree rebased on `origin/main` (`48ec58a584`).

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src` — **860/860 passing**, including the new `manager-sync-ops.prune-guard.test.ts` (both sources: failed scan ⇒ no rows pruned and `scanOk: false`; authoritatively-empty / absent-dir ⇒ orphan rows pruned) and `manager-session-sync-state.test.ts` (`pruneStaleRows` matrix).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs` on touched host-sdk files (`session-files.test.ts`, `internal.test.ts`) — passing (scan ok / authoritatively-empty / absent-dir-with-parent / unreachable-tree / failed readdir-after-lstat / failed extra path; `listSessionFilesForAgent` and `listMemoryFiles` unchanged on failure). Full `packages/memory-host-sdk` run has one unrelated under-load timeout flake in untouched `retry-utils.test.ts`; it passes in isolation.
- `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:test:packages`, `pnpm tsgo:test:extensions` — clean.
- `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:dts`, plugin-sdk boundary lints (`no-plugin-sdk-wildcard-reexports`, `no-src-outside-plugin-sdk`), `sync-plugin-sdk-exports --check` — clean.
- `oxfmt --check` + oxlint on all changed files — clean.
- Hazard proof on `main`: running this PR's failed-scan prune test against clean `origin/main` deletes the indexed session row (`expected ['sessions/main/thread.jsonl'] to deeply equal []`).

### Known gaps (CI/Testbox)

- Full `pnpm build` not run locally in the worktree — recommend CI coverage.
- `plugin-sdk:surface:check` / `plugin-sdk:api:check` not re-run locally after the final rebase (both were failing on pre-rebase `main` against stale baselines; `main` has since regenerated them). If CI flags the two intentional new exports (`scanSessionFilesForAgent`, `scanMemoryFiles`), a `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen` baseline bump can ride along.

## Notes

- #91081 (session-listing cache perf PR, still open) is complementary: it reduces READDIR load; this PR makes the destructive consumers stop trusting failed listings. No conflicts beyond both touching `session-files.ts` (additive here).

target.title: fix(cli-runner): scope claude-cli queue to live-session owner identity (#91946)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #91946. Replaces the workspace-scoped queue key that fresh `claude-cli` runs share with the same owner identity that `claude-live-session.ts` already uses for its live-session map. Independent OpenClaw sessions sharing one workspace can now run concurrently while resume safety for the same session is unchanged.

## Approach

Per ClawSweeper review on #91946 (the bot recommended keying at the existing live-session ownership boundary):

- New `buildClaudeOwnerKey({agentAccountId, agentId, authProfileId, sessionId, sessionKey})` helper in `src/agents/cli-runner/helpers.ts`.
- `resolveCliRunQueueKey()` now prefers `:owner:<ownerKey>` over `:workspace:<dir>` when no `cliSessionId` is present. Workspace fallback retained for callers that lack owner identity.
- `buildClaudeLiveKey()` in `claude-live-session.ts` refactored to share the same helper so queue identity and live-session identity stay aligned.
- `execute.ts` call site updated to pass owner identity from `context.params` + `context.effectiveAuthProfileId`.

## Real behavior proof — exact-head head

This proof exercises the **exact owner-key implementation in this PR head** (commit `c74180f8`), running both the byte-identical helpers from `src/agents/cli-runner/helpers.ts` and `src/plugin-sdk/keyed-async-queue.ts` against a microsecond-stamped workload that demonstrates the full concurrency contract: BEFORE collapse, distinct-owner overlap, and same-owner serialization in one run.

**Demo script:** [`scripts/demo-91946-queue.mjs`](https://github.com/wangwllu/openclaw/blob/fix/91946-cli-queue-owner-key/scripts/demo-91946-queue.mjs) — pure Node, no test runner. The script reads the PR-head source files, slices out the helpers, prints sha256 fingerprints of those slices, then exercises an inline copy of the same helpers (the inline copy is byte-equivalent to the slices it fingerprints — anyone running it locally will see identical hashes).

**Run output (redacted), Node 26.0.0, macOS Darwin 25.4.0 (arm64), 2026-06-11:**

```
=== PR #91974 live queue concurrency demo ===
source-slice fingerprints (sha256, first 16 hex):
  buildClaudeOwnerKey      ca630bbe8a8006ed
  resolveCliRunQueueKey    2093153b76387144
  enqueueCliRun            202b9db670beedb6
  KeyedAsyncQueue          c5bf77c1fd1c3bf8

queue-key resolution at PR head:
  session A owner-hash         dbb0a644265584efb331faba467b8339411f0cd1877d205bf96f80cc60ba34df
  session B owner-hash         3690d4faf0e6abd8a7a26c5f260938b8aa348df7e2459e68dcced7bc7cb40e43
  -> queueKey(session A)       claude-cli:owner:dbb0a644265584efb331faba467b8339411f0cd1877d205bf96f80cc60ba34df
  -> queueKey(session B)       claude-cli:owner:3690d4faf0e6abd8a7a26c5f260938b8aa348df7e2459e68dcced7bc7cb40e43
  -> queueKey(no owner) [main] claude-cli:workspace:/Users/redacted/openclaw
  ownerKey distinct?           true
  queueKey distinct?           true

event log (ms since demo start):
  t=   2.13  start  BEFORE.A.r1
  t= 102.99  end    BEFORE.A.r1
  t= 103.01  start  BEFORE.B.r1     ← BEFORE: distinct sessions collapse to workspace lane → serial
  t= 204.23  end    BEFORE.B.r1
  t= 204.51  start  A.r1
  t= 204.57  start  B.r1            ← AFTER: distinct owners → independent lanes → overlap
  t= 306.07  end    A.r1
  t= 306.46  end    B.r1
  t= 306.92  start  A.r2

  t= 509.73  end    A.r3
  t= 509.74  start  A.r4
  t= 610.97  end    A.r4

phase 0 wall (BEFORE: collapsed serial ≈ 200ms): 202ms
phase 1 wall (AFTER  overlap expected ≈ 100ms):  102ms
phase 2 wall (AFTER  serial  expected ≈ 300ms):  304ms

BEFORE-PR collapse reproduced:    PASS
distinct-owner overlap:           PASS
identical-owner serialization:    PASS
```

What this establishes against the PR-head implementation:

1. **BEFORE-PR collapse reproduced.** With no `ownerKey`, two distinct sessions in one workspace fall to `claude-cli:workspace:<dir>` and serialize (203 ms wall for two 100 ms tasks).
2. **Distinct-owner overlap.** Two sessions with different `(agentAccountId, agentId, authProfileId, sessionId, sessionKey)` tuples produce distinct sha256 owner hashes, distinct queue keys, and overlap (102 ms wall for two parallel 100 ms tasks).
3. **Identical-owner serialization.** Three fresh runs sharing one owner tuple (no `cliSessionId`, simulating same-OpenClaw-session re-entry between turns) all land on the same `claude-cli:owner:<hash>` lane and serialize end-to-end (304 ms wall for three sequential 100 ms tasks).

The owner-key fallback is preferred over workspace fallback only when an owner is supplied, and the existing `cliSessionId` precedence is unaffected (already covered by the unit-test `prefers cliSessionId over ownerKey`).

## Real behavior proof — end-to-end fan-out

(retained from the previous review round as supplementary evidence; the exact-head exercise above is the primary proof.)

- **Behavior addressed:** Fresh `claude-cli` `sessions_spawn` children sharing one OpenClaw workspace serialized through a single `KeyedAsyncQueue` lane keyed by workspace path, so configured `subagents.maxConcurrent` fan-out collapsed to ~12-second cadence regardless of how many children were spawned.

- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw v2026.6.5 install (`/Users/wanglu241/.local/lib/node_modules/openclaw`) on macOS Darwin 25.4.0 (arm64), Node 26.0.0, with `agents.defaults.subagents.maxConcurrent: 12` and a real `claude-cli/Claude-Opus-4.7-hq` provider routed via the bundled Anthropic CLI runtime. The fix shape in this PR was applied as a 1-line `dist` shim (`resolveCliRunQueueKey` returns `:run:<runId>` instead of `:workspace:<dir>`) so the same code path could run end-to-end without a rebuild; the shim is functionally equivalent to this PR's owner-key fallback for the spawn case.

- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
  1. Patched `helpers-DmEWhF5F.js:212` in the installed `openclaw` dist to drop the workspace fallback (per-run key fallback). `openclaw gateway restart`. Confirmed `gateway status` running, probe ok.
  2. Issued five rounds of `sessions_spawn` fan-out from one parent OpenClaw session, each child a 5-second `python3` print/sleep/print probe. Rounds: N=2 (PROBE-N2-X/Y), N=3 (PROBE-V1..V3), N=4 (PROBE-W1..W4), N=6 (PROBE-X1..X6), N=8 (PROBE-Y1..Y8). 23 children total.
  3. Captured `cli exec` rows, child start/end timestamps, and tool-call counts from `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-2026-06-10.log`.
  4. As a counter-baseline reference, the same 4-spawn shape was run before patching with a `cliqueue-probe enqueueCliRun` shim and produced the strict 12-second serial cadence captured in #91946.

- **Evidence:** Pre-patch baseline — workspace lane serializes fresh runs, captured in #91946 [#issuecomment-4670918725](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/91946#issuecomment-4670918725):

  ```
  21:43:49.737  cliqueue-probe  enqueueCliRun key=claude-cli:workspace:/Users/wanglu241/.openclaw/workspace
  21:43:49…     cli exec  ← child A
  21:44:01…     cli exec  ← child B  (~12.3 s after A)
  21:44:13…     cli exec  ← child C  (~12.0 s after B)
  21:44:25…     cli exec  ← child D  (~12.1 s after C)
  ```

  Post-patch fan-out, redacted log slice for the N=8 round (children Y1..Y8 dispatched within a 9-second wall-clock window — pre-patch this would have taken ~96 s):

  ```
  PROBE-Y1 start=23:23:34.123  end=23:23:39.180   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y2 start=23:23:35.259  end=23:23:40.294   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y3 start=23:23:36.090  end=23:23:41.134   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y4 start=23:23:38.505  end=23:23:43.556   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y5 start=23:23:38.349  end=23:23:43.399   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y6 start=23:23:40.267  end=23:23:45.325   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y7 start=23:23:41.326  end=23:23:46.383   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y8 start=23:23:43.156  end=23:23:48.209   tool_uses=1
  ```

  Stress matrix across all five rounds (linked in [#issuecomment-4671803091](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/91946#issuecomment-4671803091)):

  | round | N | success | dispatch span | retries |
  |---|---|---|---|---|
  | N=2 | 2 | 2/2 | 2.0 s | 0 |
  | N=3 | 3 | 3/3 | 2.0 s | 0 |
  | N=4 | 4 | 4/4 | — | 1 (unrelated macOS `date '+%3N'` quirk inside the child agent) |
  | N=6 | 6 | 6/6 | 8.2 s | 0 |
  | N=8 | 8 | 8/8 | 9.0 s | 0 |
  | total | 23 | 23/23 | — | 0 cli backend retries |

- **Observed result after the fix:** Independent fresh `claude-cli` `sessions_spawn` children in one workspace now dispatch their `cli exec` rows within ~1 s of each other instead of every ~12 s, and 23/23 children completed cleanly across 5 fan-out rounds. Same-OpenClaw-session resumed runs continue to serialize per `cliSessionId` (unchanged path), and `buildClaudeLiveKey()` now derives from the same helper so the queue key cannot drift from the live-session lifecycle key.

- **What was not tested:**
  - Multi-workspace concurrent fan-out (the bug only manifested when sharing one workspace; cross-workspace already had distinct keys, no regression risk for that path).
  - Codex / OpenCode CLI backends (`isClaudeCliProvider` short-circuits this code path; their queue key is unchanged).
  - Resume-after-crash race against an in-flight fresh run for the same OpenClaw session (this PR keeps `cliSessionId` precedence over `ownerKey`, so the existing per-session serialization for resume holds; the precedence is exercised by the new "prefers cliSessionId over ownerKey" unit test).

## Open questions for maintainer

1. Should the workspace fallback be kept (current PR) or removed? Removing tightens the contract but breaks any caller that doesn't have owner identity yet.
2. `buildClaudeOwnerKey` currently uses the same JSON.stringify field order as `buildClaudeLiveKey`'s previous inline expression. If you'd prefer canonical key sorting, happy to switch.
3. Is there a reason `execute.ts` shouldn't have access to `context.effectiveAuthProfileId` here? It's read for the live-session path already, so this PR assumes the same exposure is fine for the queue key.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 ✓ cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw cron run` (manual) of a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job incorrectly deletes the job. Manual runs also pollute `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `0cec2acc7a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, counter mutations and `shouldDelete` run unconditionally — no `isManual` discriminator exists.
- **Exact steps after patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` + `src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 46/46 timer regression tests pass (including 7 new #83538/#83933-specific tests). 7/7 failure-alert tests pass.
- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI flow; the tests exercise the same `applyJobResult` code path the Gateway calls internally.

### Key test results

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun + success | NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun + success | Deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |
| Manual error → failure alert | NOT triggered ✅ |
| Scheduled error → failure alert | Triggered ✅ |


## Real behavior proof (Docker, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker container, `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched source at `885d605e56`.

### Latest fixes

| Issue | Problem | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| P1 | Manual at-job error was disabling job (`enabled=false`, `nextRunAtMs=undefined`) | At-job error retry/disable block now guarded with `if (opts?.isManual)` |
| P2 | Manual success was clearing `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown | Cooldown clearing now inside `if (!opts?.isManual)` guard |

### Test results

| Test Suite | Result |
|------------|--------|
| Timer regression (49 tests) | ✅ All passed |
| Failure-alert (7 tests) | ✅ All passed |

### Key verified behaviors

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual at-job error | `enabled` stays true, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| Manual success | `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown preserved ✅ |
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun | Job NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun | Job deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |


## Real CLI proof (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway at `885d605e56` running with `--allow-unconfigured`.

**Terminal transcript**: `openclaw cron add` → `cron show` → `cron run` (manual, triggers error) → `cron show` (job still exists, enabled=true, nextRunAtMs preserved) → `cron list` (job present) → `cron runs` (run history with error status, nextRunAtMs unchanged).

| Step | Command | Result |
|------|---------|--------|
| Add job | `openclaw cron add --name proof-job --at ... --delete-after-run` | Created with `deleteAfterRun: true` |
| Manual run | `openclaw cron run <id>` | `{"ok": true, "enqueued": true}` |
| After run | `openclaw cron show <id>` | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |
| Job exists | `openclaw cron list` | Job still listed ✅ |
| Run history | `openclaw cron runs --id <id>` | `status: "error"`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |


## Real CLI proof at head SHA `642c3450d9` (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway built from PR head.

| Test | Command | Before (main) | After (PR head) |
|------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| A: at-job deleteAfterRun | `cron run` → `cron show` | Job deleted/disabled | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| B: every-job manual error | `cron run` → `cron show` | `nextRunAtMs` rewritten by backoff | `nextRunAtMs` unchanged ✅ |
| A+B: counters | `cron runs` | `consecutiveErrors` mutated | Unchanged ✅ |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, or failure-alert cooldown.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `3d32c9baff`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive `deleteAfterRun`, counter mutations, at-job disable, recurring backoff, and alert cooldown. The 12 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or commands run after this patch**:

```bash
# Build Docker image from PR HEAD
docker build --target build -t openclaw-pr83933:test .

# Test 1: Timer regression tests (includes all 12 manual-run isolation tests)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

# Test 2: Failure alert tests (adapted for manual-run isolation)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts

# Test 3: Lint check
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test npx oxlint src/cron/service/timer.ts src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts src/cron/service/ops.ts

# Test 4: Full cron test suite
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

Test 1 — Timer regression: **55 tests passed** (284ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (55 tests) 284ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  55 passed (55)
```

Test 2 — Failure alert: **10 tests passed** (63ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 63ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  10 passed (10)
```

Test 3 — Lint: **0 errors, 0 warnings**
```
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Finished in 13ms on 4 files with 189 rules using 10 threads.
```

Test 4 — Full cron suite: **1000 tests passed** across 98 test files
```
 Test Files  1 failed | 97 passed (98)
      Tests  2 failed | 1000 passed (1002)
```
The 2 failures are in `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts` — these look for Swift source files (`apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/CronModels.swift`) which are not present in the Docker build target. These failures are pre-existing and unrelated to this PR.

- **Observed result after fix**: All 12 new regression tests pass, confirming that manual runs preserve one-shot jobs (`deleteAfterRun`), do not increment/reset counters, do not disable at-jobs on error, do not rewrite recurring `nextRunAtMs` with backoff, and do not clear failure-alert cooldown. Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all 5 state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting-only changes via oxfmt). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, 2026-05-30)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, failure-alert cooldown, or skipped-run alert counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive all 5 scheduled-state effects plus skipped-run alert paths. The 14 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or commands run after this patch**:

```bash
# Build Docker image from PR HEAD
docker build --target build -t openclaw-pr83933:test .

# Test 1: Timer regression tests (all 14 manual-run isolation tests)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

# Test 2: Failure alert tests (adapted for manual-run isolation)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts

# Test 3: Lint check
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test npx oxlint src/cron/service/timer.ts src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts src/cron/service/ops.ts

# Test 4: Full cron test suite
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

Test 1 — Timer regression: **56 tests passed** (234ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (56 tests) 234ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  56 passed (56)
```

Test 2 — Failure alert: **11 tests passed** (92ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (11 tests) 92ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)
```

Test 3 — Lint: **0 errors, 0 warnings**
```
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Finished in 10ms on 4 files with 189 rules using 10 threads.
```

Test 4 — Full cron suite: **1002 tests passed** across 98 test files
```
 Test Files  1 failed | 97 passed (98)
      Tests  2 failed | 1002 passed (1004)
```
The 2 failures are in `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts` — these look for Swift source files (`apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/CronModels.swift`) which are not present in the Docker build target. Pre-existing and unrelated to this PR.

- **Observed result after fix**: All 14 new regression tests pass, covering all 6 guarded state effects including the newly-added manual skipped-run isolation:
  - Manual `deleteAfterRun` at-job → NOT deleted ✅
  - Manual error → counters NOT incremented ✅
  - Manual success → counters NOT reset, cooldown NOT cleared ✅
  - Manual at-job error → NOT disabled, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅
  - Manual every-job error → backoff NOT applied, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅
  - Manual skipped at-job → consecutiveSkipped NOT incremented, alert NOT fired, all state preserved ✅
  - Manual skipped run with `includeSkipped: true` → failure alert NOT emitted ✅

  Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting + test coverage changes). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.

### Manual-run state contract

`cron run` is a debug/verification command. Manual runs record their outcome (lastRunAtMs, lastStatus, lastError) but do not advance scheduled state. This makes manual runs safe, non-destructive operations that cannot interfere with scheduled execution. No reasonable user scenario depends on manual runs consuming one-shot jobs or driving retry/backoff state.

## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`, 2026-06-02)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `lastRunWasManual` field was missing from the `CronJobStateSchema` and `CronJobStatePatchSchema` in `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/cron.ts`. Both schemas use `additionalProperties: false`, so returning a cron job state containing `lastRunWasManual` would fail gateway protocol validation on `cron list`/`cron show`. Added `lastRunWasManual: Type.Optional(Type.Boolean())` to both schemas.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`), Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `ccb89e3f11`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On the previous commit (`45efd1abdd`), `CronJobStateSchema` did not include `lastRunWasManual`. Any cron job state object with `lastRunWasManual: true` (set by `timer.ts:414` after a manual run) would be rejected by TypeBox validation due to `additionalProperties: false`. The `CronJobStatePatchSchema` was similarly missing the field, preventing state patches that include it.

- **Exact steps or commands run after this patch**:

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" \
  -v /app/node_modules \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" \
  node:24-bookworm \
  bash -c '
    cd /app && npm install 2>&1 | tail -3 && \
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
      src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts 2>&1'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (56 tests) 335ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts (15 tests) 54ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 31ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts (3 tests) 3ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts (2 tests) 0ms

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  86 passed (86)
   Start at  07:37:28
   Duration  3.44s (transform 2.31s, setup 280ms, import 2.62s, tests 424ms, environment 0ms)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 86 tests pass including:
  - `timer.regression.test.ts`: 56 tests — manual run sets `lastRunWasManual: true` on state (line 2531), state now passes schema validation.
  - `service.restart-catchup.test.ts`: 15 tests — restart catch-up checks `lastRunWasManual` in state fixture (line 224) and correctly re-runs the job.
  - `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts`: 3 tests — schema conformance verified (delivery modes, status shape, failover reasons).
  - `cron-protocol-schema.test.ts`: 2 tests — schema structure validated (deprecated alias, failure notification state).

- **What was not tested**: Live `cron list`/`cron show` gateway roundtrip with the patched schema. The schema fix is structural (adding a field to a TypeBox object with `additionalProperties: false`) and the test suite validates that state objects with `lastRunWasManual` are accepted by the schema.

## Live Gateway CLI roundtrip proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job must not delete the job, must not disable it, must not clear `nextRunAtMs`, and must set `lastRunWasManual: true` in state. The `lastRunWasManual` field must pass `CronJobStateSchema` validation (which uses `additionalProperties: false`).
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64, OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, OpenClaw Gateway built from source at commit `ccb89e3f11`.
- **Steps**:
  1. Built OpenClaw from source on the PR branch
  2. Started Gateway inside Docker with `gateway run --dev --allow-unconfigured --auth token`
  3. Created a `deleteAfterRun` at-job via `cron add`
  4. Manually ran the job via `cron run` (resulted in error due to missing codex-linux-arm64 in Docker — this is the interesting case: manual error on a deleteAfterRun job)
  5. Verified job survival, `lastRunWasManual: true`, `enabled: true`, and `nextRunAtMs` preserved via `cron show` and `cron list`
  6. Verified `cron list` and `cron show` returned the `lastRunWasManual` field without schema validation errors

- **Observed result**: After manual `cron run` (which triggered an error due to missing codex dependency in Docker):
  - Job **survived** — still present in `cron list` and `cron show` ✅
  - `enabled: true` — manual error did NOT disable the at-job ✅
  - `nextRunAtMs: 1798732799000` — preserved, NOT cleared ✅
  - `deleteAfterRun: true` — still set, NOT consumed by manual run ✅
  - `lastRunWasManual: true` — new field present and accepted by schema ✅
  - No `consecutiveErrors` field — manual error did NOT increment counter ✅
  - Run history shows `runId: "manual:..."` prefix confirming manual execution path ✅
  - Both `cron show` and `cron list` returned `lastRunWasManual` without schema validation errors, proving `CronJobStateSchema` correctly includes the field ✅

- **What was not tested**: Manual run with `status: "ok"` (would require a working LLM provider in Docker). The `shouldDelete` guard path (`wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual`) is exercised by unit tests. Multi-node cluster, production load.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `8596607fca`, 2026-06-02 — backoff fix)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a recurring job that errors must not open a backoff window that blocks the next scheduled fire. Previously, `isErrorBackoffPending()`, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()`, `isPendingErrorBackoffSlot()`, and `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` read `lastRunStatus` and `lastRunAtMs` without checking `lastRunWasManual`, causing a manual error to push `nextRunAtMs` forward and block the timer-scheduled fire.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `8596607fca`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` does not check `lastRunWasManual`. A recurring every-job with `lastRunWasManual: true` and `lastRunStatus: "error"` would have `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` return a backoff deadline, `isErrorBackoffPending()` would return `true`, and `isRunnableJob()` would return `false` — blocking the next scheduled fire. `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` would also push `nextRunAtMs` forward to the backoff floor. Test file authored as part of this PR.

- **Exact steps or commands run after this patch**:

```bash
# Unit tests in Docker
docker run --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" -e CI=true \
  node:24-bookworm bash -c \
  "node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts"

# Live Gateway CLI in Docker
docker run --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" -e CI=true \
  -e OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-test \
  -e OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=test-token-123 \
  node:24-bookworm bash -c '
    mkdir -p /tmp/openclaw-test
    echo "{\"gateway\":{\"port\":19001,\"bind\":\"loopback\"}}" > /tmp/openclaw-test/openclaw.json
    node openclaw.mjs gateway run --dev --allow-unconfigured --token test-token-123 --port 19001 &
    sleep 8
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-at-delete --at "2026-06-02T23:00:00Z" --message reminder --delete-after-run --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>, cron list
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-recurring --every 10s --message "recurring check" --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>
    kill %1'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```

 ✓ cron src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts (15 tests) 44ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 25ms

 Test Files  3 passed (3)

   Start at  11:08:37
   Duration  2.77s (transform 1.83s, setup 250ms, import 2.09s, tests 323ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 5.85s
```

</details>

<details>

```

v24.16.0

[gateway] ready

TEST 1: Create deleteAfterRun at-job
  → id: dbfbe16b-c44a-4aa1-b820-889dbdbcb9a2, deleteAfterRun: true, nextRunAtMs: 1780441200000

  → { "ok": true, "enqueued": true, "runId": "manual:dbfbe16b-..." }

  → status: "running" (agent executing but will error — no LLM provider in Docker)
```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. **deleteAfterRun at-job manual run**: Job survived manual run, `enabled: true`, `deleteAfterRun: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅
  2. **Recurring job manual run**: Job's `nextRunAtMs` preserved at natural cadence, NOT pushed forward by backoff ✅
  3. **Schema validation**: Both `cron list --json` and `cron show --json` returned `lastRunWasManual` field without validation errors ✅
  4. **New regression test**: `#83933: manual error on recurring job does not block scheduled fire via backoff` — verifies `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` returns `undefined` for manual errors, `isRunnableJob()` returns `true` within theoretical backoff window, `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance()` computes natural cadence (not backoff floor), and control case confirms scheduled errors still apply backoff ✅

- **What was not tested**: Manual run with `status: "ok"` on recurring job (requires working LLM in Docker). Multi-node cluster, production load, actual delivery to chat channels.

target.title: fix(channel): harden local setup trust
target.body:
## Summary
- follow up on #86953's workspace-only setup trust fix by extending the same trusted-catalog fallback logic to local `config` / `global` channel plugins discovered through `plugins.load.paths` or linked installs
- prove the regression on current code with the blackbox plugin-trust harness: clean `main` blocks trusted `plugins.load.paths` setup because the catalog never sees that channel, while this branch reaches the loader and passes all four trust cases
- keep the setup trust rule origin-aware: reject untrusted local shadows by `{ pluginId, origin }`, then fall back to a safe bundled/non-local copy instead of suppressing all copies of the same plugin id
- fix the `.dts` build break in `src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.ts`, which was failing fresh `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:dts` / Docker image builds even when local incremental caches hid it
- add main-repo L4 coverage plus a focused Docker/package L6 manual proof script for the local channel plugin trust boundary

## Change Type
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs / PR context
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore / infra

## Scope
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI / infra
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage

## Linked Issue / PR
- Follow-up to merged #86953 (`fix(plugins): block untrusted workspace setup-only channel loads`)
- Context and autoreview notes: `my_docs/my_docs/04-cases/2026-04-10-channel-workspace-shadow-case/pr-86953-autoreview-follow-up.zh.md`
- This PR exists because #86953 fixed the workspace trust boundary, but the same local-code trust question still applied to `plugins.load.paths` (`origin: "config"`) and linked/local installs that surface as `origin: "global"`

## Problem Statement
#86953 established the product rule that untrusted local channel plugins should not get imported during setup-only channel flows. That landed for workspace-origin plugins.

The remaining gap was the broader local-origin surface:
- `plugins.load.paths` channel plugins appear as `origin: "config"`
- linked / managed local installs can appear as `origin: "global"`

Those are still local code from the operator's point of view. If setup hardening only keys on `origin === "workspace"`, the trust boundary is inconsistent: one class of local plugin is blocked before import, while other local shadows can still win channel catalog resolution or suppress a safe bundled fallback.

This branch also had a separate concrete defect in fresh builds: `src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.ts` failed declaration generation, so Docker image builds died in `build:plugin-sdk:dts` even though local incremental caches could mask the problem.

The fresh-build failure was:

```text
src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.ts(82,3): error TS2344:
Type '"excludeOrigins" | "excludePluginRefs"' does not satisfy the constraint 'never'.
src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.ts(97,3): error TS2322:
Type '{ excludeOrigins?: (PluginOrigin | undefined)[] | undefined; ... }'
is not assignable to type 'Pick<CatalogOptions | undefined, "excludeOrigins" | "excludePluginRefs">'.
src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.ts(113,61): error TS2345:
Argument of type '{ ... excludeOrigins: unknown; excludePluginRefs: unknown; }'
is not assignable to parameter of type 'CatalogOptions'.
```

## Blackbox Proof
Harness:
- e2e repo: sibling `openclaw-e2e-go`
- spec: `specs/plugintrust/local_origin_followup_test.go`
- probe: `scripts/docker/test-plugintrust-local-origin.sh`
- fixtures: `scripts/fixtures/plugintrust/{workspace-shadow,load-paths-shadow}/`
- command: `OPENCLAW_E2E_TARGET_REPO=<openclaw checkout> go test ./specs/plugintrust -v`

The harness bakes a fresh OpenClaw image from source, then asserts only on marker files written by fixture plugins:
- `PLUGINTRUST_SETUP_IMPORT_MARKER`: module top-level import happened
- `PLUGINTRUST_SETUP_REGISTER_MARKER`: setup hook actually ran

Clean `main` (`a4196a44454f`) behavior:
- Case 1 `workspace + untrusted`: PASS, trust gate blocks the untrusted workspace plugin
- Case 2 `workspace + plugins.allow`: PASS, trusted workspace plugin loads
- Case 3 `load-paths + untrusted`: PASS as probe-only, marker absent because catalog rejects `Unknown channel e2e-load-paths` before loader
- Case 4 `load-paths + plugins.allow`: FAIL, same `Unknown channel e2e-load-paths`; trusted load-path plugin is still invisible to the catalog on main

This branch behavior from the previously built fresh image:
- Case 1: PASS, untrusted workspace shadow not loaded
- Case 2: PASS, trusted workspace shadow loaded
- Case 3: PASS, load-path probe reached and recorded
- Case 4: PASS, trusted load-path plugin loaded

That is the direct before/after proof for the regression this PR fixes: on `main`, trusted `plugins.load.paths` channels never reach setup because the catalog cannot resolve them; on this branch, the same scenario resolves and loads correctly while the untrusted cases still stay blocked.

## What Changed
1. Thread `extraPaths` through channel catalog resolution so trusted channel setup/add flows can actually see channel plugins discovered from `plugins.load.paths`.
2. Make fallback exclusion origin-aware with `excludePluginRefs: [{ pluginId, origin }]`, so rejecting an untrusted local shadow only skips that exact local candidate and still allows a bundled copy of the same channel/plugin id to win.
3. Keep setup discovery lenient for display-only cases: if an untrusted local entry has no trusted fallback, it stays visible in the setup discovery bucket rather than disappearing from the UI.
4. Fix the TypeScript declaration-build typing in `trusted-catalog.ts` by replacing the unstable `Pick<Parameters<...>[1], ...>` shape with an explicit local exclusions type and explicit local-origin narrowing.
5. Add `src/commands/channels.add.trust.e2e.test.ts` as a main-repo L4 E2E regression for the real `channels add` CLI path.
6. Add `bash scripts/e2e/channel-plugin-trust-docker.sh` as a focused Docker/package L6 manual proof script for the `plugins.load.paths` trust boundary.

## Why This Is The Right Follow-up To #86953
#86953 already proved the core trust model: local untrusted channel plugin code must not execute in setup-only flows. This PR does not redefine that model; it applies the same invariant consistently to the remaining local origins the earlier PR intentionally left as a follow-up hardening item.

The `pr-86953-autoreview-follow-up.zh.md` note called out exactly this direction: stop hardcoding one origin name and move the loader/catalog gate toward a unified local trust decision. This PR stays within that scope and keeps the current product behavior for trusted plugins:
- explicitly trusted / allowlisted local plugins still resolve
- bundled entries still act as the safe fallback when a local shadow is not trusted
- discovery UI can still show installable local-only candidates without treating them as executable

## Verification
- `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:dts`
- `pnpm test src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm test src/commands/channels.add.trust.e2e.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`

target.title: fix(clickclack): allow explicit enable through plugin allowlist
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix ClickClack enablement when `plugins.allow` is already a non-empty restrictive list.
- Root cause: explicit CLI/setup/onboarding selections used the generic fail-closed enable helper, which rejected ClickClack before the selection could be recorded as trust.
- Add a narrow explicit-selection path that appends only canonical `clickclack` after an allowlist rejection. Global plugin disablement, `plugins.deny`, and all unrelated plugin IDs remain authoritative.
- Wire that path into `plugins enable`, guided channel setup, and onboarding installation. Normal direct plugin-install policy is unchanged.
- Out of scope: ClickClack's GitHub organization login gate and any broad change to OpenClaw plugin allowlist policy.

## Linked context

Related: https://github.com/openclaw/clickclack/issues/16

Requested and behavior-authorized by the owner.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw plugins enable clickclack` no longer reports `blocked by allowlist` solely because another plugin already occupies `plugins.allow`.
- Real environment tested: built OpenClaw CLI from final commit `24af9d8e751440a8954aa731c5d2c63a53094698`, using isolated disposable `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` values.
- Exact commands run after this patch: `pnpm build`; then built CLI invocations for `plugins enable clickclack`, `plugins enable telegram`, denylisted `plugins enable clickclack`, and `plugins install clickclack` against four separate configs.
- Evidence after fix:
  - ClickClack enable: `Enabled plugin "clickclack".`; config became `allow: ["memory-core", "clickclack"]` with the ClickClack entry enabled.
  - Unrelated Telegram enable: `blocked by allowlist`; config stayed `allow: ["memory-core"]`.
  - Denylisted ClickClack enable: `blocked by denylist`; allowlist and denylist stayed unchanged.
  - Bundled ClickClack install: install completed and recorded ClickClack in the existing allowlist.
- Observed result after fix: explicit ClickClack trust is materialized without weakening policy for other plugin IDs or overriding deny/global-disable checks.
- What was not tested: a live ClickClack bot connection; this change owns config mutation before channel runtime startup and needs no service token or network call.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: none for the affected OpenClaw config boundary.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm test src/plugins/enable.test.ts src/flows/channel-setup.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.policy.test.ts src/commands/onboarding-plugin-install.test.ts` (65 tests passed on the final commit)
- `pnpm build` (passed on the final commit)
- Autoreview: clean twice; no accepted/actionable findings.
- Blacksmith Testbox through Crabbox `tbx_01kttbpjd3gshqf74a93hskew7`: changed-file formatting, `pnpm check --include-test-types`, and `pnpm check:docs` passed. Run: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27321882027
- The same run executed all 88 full-test shards. 87 shards passed; the untouched `agents-core` shard has two current-main failures that reproduce locally in `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts` and `src/agents/simple-completion-runtime.test.ts`.
- Repository-wide `pnpm format:check` also reports 24 pre-existing files outside this patch; all changed files pass formatting. Baseline run: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27321833711

Regression coverage verifies ClickClack append-and-enable behavior, unrelated-plugin rejection, denylist preservation, and the guided channel setup path.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes`, only explicit ClickClack selection can append its ID to an existing restrictive allowlist.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `Yes`, at the plugin trust-policy boundary.

Highest-risk area: accidentally broadening allowlist bypass behavior.

Mitigation: the retry is limited to canonical `clickclack` and only after the generic helper returns `blocked by allowlist`; global disablement and denylist checks run first, and unrelated-ID regression coverage remains fail-closed.

## Current review state

Ready for Peter's review. Do not merge until owner review is complete. No known patch-specific blocker remains; the full-suite and repository-wide formatting failures listed above are reproducible untouched-main baseline failures.

target.title: fix(agent): use static catalog for skip-agent model resolution
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes the skip-agent-discovery model-resolution path so known provider-owned models can use the bundled static catalog before OpenClaw models.json is generated.
- This prevents known Google Vertex model refs such as google-vertex/gemini-2.5-pro from surfacing a misleading missing models.providers registration error when the static catalog already knows the model.
- Adds regression coverage for the Google Vertex run path.

## Linked context
Closes #88816

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)
- Behavior or issue addressed: google-vertex/gemini-2.5-pro could fail before reply with an Unknown model / missing provider registration error while skip-agent-discovery resolution was active.
- Real environment tested: main-based OpenClaw gwt on local macOS, focused runner test harness; live Google Vertex API credentials were not present.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.test.ts
- Evidence after fix: focused Vitest passed: 2 files, 219 tests, including the Google Vertex skip-agent-discovery static catalog regression plus resolver fallback coverage.
- Observed result after fix: skip-agent-discovery resolution is permitted to consult the bundled static catalog for known model refs.
- What was not tested: a live Google Vertex API-key request against the external service.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: GOOGLE_CLOUD_API_KEY, GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS, GEMINI_API_KEY, GEMINI_LIVE_TEST, and OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST were unset locally, so a live external Google Vertex call remains a maintainer/reporter proof gap.
- Before evidence: issue #88816 includes the reported failing error for google-vertex/gemini-2.5-pro and google-vertex/gemini-3.5-flash.

## Tests and validation
- PNPM_CONFIG_OFFLINE=true corepack pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts -- --reporter=dot
- corepack pnpm oxfmt src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts (via commit hook)
- git diff --check

## Risk checklist
- Did user-visible behavior change? Yes, known static-catalog models can resolve earlier in the skip-agent-discovery path instead of waiting for OpenClaw model discovery or erroring.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No secrets or network behavior changes; this only allows local bundled model metadata fallback during model resolution.
- Highest-risk area: resolving a static-catalog model slightly earlier than before. Mitigation: the fallback only applies to known bundled catalog entries, and existing full model discovery remains available for runtime-generated models.

## Current review state
- Next action: maintainer review and, ideally, reporter/maintainer confirmation with a real Google Vertex API-key profile.
- Waiting on: CI and any maintainer-requested real-provider proof.
- Bot/reviewer comments addressed: pushed a main-based maintainer refresh at de9bb4944aa with the existing source fix plus regression coverage; live-provider proof remains blocked by missing credentials.
