target.title: fix(gateway): gate cron on channel readiness
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Prevent gateway cron from starting delivery work before configured channel transports report ready during startup and hot reload.

Why does this matter now?

- Cron catch-up can immediately deliver notifications after gateway startup or config reload; Discord can be running before it is connected.

What is the intended outcome?

- Cron waits for running configured channel accounts with explicit `connected` state, then fail-opens after the existing channel connect grace timeout with a warning.
- Hot reload restarts cron after channel restart completes, and restores the old cron if channel restart fails after the old cron was stopped.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No plugin changes, config changes, migrations, or delivery retry policy changes.

What does success look like?

- Startup and hot-reload cron paths do not race known channel readiness, without blocking disabled, unconfigured, stopped, skipped, or closing gateways.

What should reviewers focus on?

- `src/gateway/server-runtime-services.ts` and `src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.ts` lifecycle ordering around cron start, channel readiness, skipped channels, and rollback on channel restart failure.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #62891

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Maintainer-requested local fix pass.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: gateway cron startup and hot-reload cron restart now wait for running configured channel accounts with explicit `connected` state before starting cron, with timeout fail-open and skipped-channel bypasses.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, Node runtime executing the gateway startup and hot-reload paths from this branch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/cron-channel-readiness.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-cron.test.ts src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts src/gateway/server-import-boundary.test.ts
node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/cron-channel-readiness.ts src/gateway/cron-channel-readiness.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts src/gateway/server.impl.ts
node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/gateway/cron-channel-readiness.ts src/gateway/cron-channel-readiness.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts src/gateway/server.impl.ts
git diff --check
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit --pretty false
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 3.30s
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  39 passed (39)

[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 6.16s
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  23 passed (23)

Checking formatting...
All matched files use the correct format.

oxlint changed files: exit 0

git diff --check: no output

```

- Observed result after fix: post-ready cron waits until a running configured channel becomes connected; hot-reload cron starts after channel restart; skipped channel/provider envs bypass the wait; stopped accounts do not block; channel restart failure restores the old cron instead of leaving cron stopped.
- What was not tested: no live Discord gateway/SIGUSR1 timing reproduction was run.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: focused local runtime coverage only; `test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json` remains blocked by pre-existing `src/agents/agent-command.compaction-rotation.test.ts:60` type error unrelated to this diff, and broad Testbox proof was not run because `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs --help` failed local binary sanity checks.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): source review showed startup cron was scheduled after startup readiness but before channel delivery readiness, and hot reload previously restarted cron before channel restart completion.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/cron-channel-readiness.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-cron.test.ts src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts src/gateway/server-import-boundary.test.ts

node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/gateway/cron-channel-readiness.ts src/gateway/cron-channel-readiness.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts src/gateway/server.impl.ts
git diff --check
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit --pretty false
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added `cron-channel-readiness` coverage for connected, delayed connected, timeout fail-open, stopped/disabled/unconfigured/no-connected accounts, and closing gateways.
- Added post-ready cron coverage for readiness wait, timeout start, and skipped-channel bypass.
- Added hot-reload coverage for cron-after-channel ordering, skipped-channel bypass, and old-cron restore on channel restart failure.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The new ordering and rollback regression cases would fail against the previous hot-reload/startup cron behavior.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No

What is the highest-risk area?

- Startup or reload cron could be delayed when a running configured channel never reports connected.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The wait uses the existing channel connect grace timeout, fail-opens with a warning, skips disabled/unconfigured/stopped/no-readiness accounts, bypasses explicit channel/provider skip envs, and exits on gateway close.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Maintainer review and CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Live Discord/SIGUSR1 timing proof was not run locally; CI is pending.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Pre-ship round 1 MUST-FIX findings were addressed: stopped accounts no longer block readiness, skipped channel/provider envs bypass readiness, and hot-reload channel restart failure restores the old cron.

target.title: fix(update): avoid zsh readonly status in macOS handoffs
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes the macOS update/restart handoff scripts so they no longer assign to a shell variable named `status`.
- `status` is readonly in zsh, so a handoff script interpreted by zsh can stop before restart diagnostics and cleanup finish.
- Keeps the logged wording as `status=...` for operator readability while using `exit_status` internally.

## Linked context
Closes #88809

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)
- Behavior or issue addressed: macOS update handoff scripts could fail if interpreted by zsh because `status` is a readonly variable there.
- Real environment tested: local Linux contributor checkout; macOS launchd behavior was verified through generated script assertions and existing launchd/restart helper unit tests.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `corepack pnpm test src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.test.ts src/daemon/launchd-restart-handoff.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`
- Evidence after fix: the focused test command passed 2 Vitest shards, 34 tests total; new assertions check the generated macOS scripts do not contain `status=` assignments and exit with `$exit_status`.
- Observed result after fix: generated macOS restart handoff templates preserve exit-code handling without assigning to zsh's readonly `status` name.
- What was not tested: a live macOS web UI update/restart flow was not run from this Linux contributor environment.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: `zsh` is not installed in this runner, so the direct zsh readonly-variable reproduction from the issue was verified against the generated script source and protected with focused template assertions.

## Tests and validation
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.ts src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.test.ts src/daemon/launchd-restart-handoff.ts src/daemon/launchd-restart-handoff.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm test src/cli/update-cli/restart-helper.test.ts src/daemon/launchd-restart-handoff.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`
- `git diff --check`

## Risk checklist
- Did user-visible behavior change? Yes, macOS update/restart handoff scripts avoid a reserved shell variable name.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No.
- Highest-risk area: preserving restart failure exit-code propagation.
- Mitigation: existing restart-helper and launchd handoff tests still pass, including failure propagation coverage, and new assertions pin the generated macOS script shape.

## Current review state
Ready for maintainer review; no bot or reviewer comments addressed yet.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body:
## Channel echo / session pinning with native Telegram echo

This PR lets a session mirror future turns to pinned echo targets, so a webchat,
control UI, CLI, or Telegram turn can be followed from another channel that shares the
same session key.

### Behavior

- User turns are mirrored to echo targets with a source marker such as
  `[via webchat]`, so the destination can tell where the prompt originated.
- Assistant replies are mirrored as native destination-channel messages, not as
  `[echo]`-tagged copies.
- Telegram registers the first native streaming echo renderer. When a Telegram target is
  available, the source run is replayed into Telegram's own draft-stream primitive; when
  streaming is unavailable, the existing post-hoc final mirror remains the fallback.
- A streamed target is filtered out of the post-hoc mirror for that run, and direct
  `chat.send` prompt echo now marks the dispatch context so the generic hook does not
  send a second prompt copy.

### Source and test coverage

The current head is `88943686569ebff43fecc25fc51be6b9bf621e3c`. The Telegram
Desktop proof below exercises the real webchat-to-Telegram delivery path on
`e52925a313521f420f8ee8e0ac9cc6ab8c484a22`. The commits after that proof do
not change Telegram message rendering; they change the SDK/API ownership boundary and
the plugin lifecycle point where Telegram may register the renderer. Those current-head
deltas are covered by the source-level tests listed here.

- `src/infra/outbound/echo.ts` and `src/infra/outbound/echo-hook.ts`: native-final
  assistant mirror options, post-hoc fallback filtering, and duplicate prompt suppression.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`: `chat.send` direct user echo and source-reply
  assistant echo go through the same native-final / streaming-handled gate.
- `src/infra/outbound/echo-mirror-resolver.ts` and `src/infra/outbound/echo-streaming.ts`:
  runId event replay, renderer fan-out, per-run handled marks, and fallback behavior.
- `src/plugins/types.ts`, `src/plugins/api-builder.ts`, and `src/plugins/registry.ts`:
  `api.registerEchoRendererFactory(factory)` is bound by the host to the activated
  plugin's live registered channel id; plugins no longer pass or claim an arbitrary
  channel string.
- `src/plugin-sdk/channel-echo.ts`: the public `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath is
  now types-only, so it no longer exports the global registration function.
- `extensions/telegram/index.ts`, `extensions/telegram/echo-renderer-api.ts`, and
  `extensions/telegram/src/echo-renderer-register.ts`: Telegram registers its renderer
  through the owner-scoped full-runtime plugin API. Tool-discovery mode returns before
  the side effect can set the idempotency guard, so a discovery pass cannot suppress the
  later full activation.
- `extensions/telegram/src/echo-renderer*.ts` and `extensions/telegram/src/client-options.ts`:
  Telegram native renderer registration, draft-stream rendering, and reuse of Telegram
  client options.
- `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` and `docs/plugins/sdk-subpaths.md`: docs describe the
  owner-scoped activation API and the types-only channel echo subpath.

Commands run locally on the current head after the owner-binding and lifecycle fixes:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/index.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/echo-renderer-register.test.ts src/plugins/registry.runtime-config.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-subpaths.test.ts src/infra/outbound/echo-streaming.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:extensions
pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm build:plugin-sdk:dts
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
git diff --check
```

Earlier focused validation for the duplicate-suppression and Telegram-rendering fixes
also passed:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/echo-renderer-register.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/echo-renderer.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.proxy.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.send-user-echo.test.ts src/infra/outbound/echo-hook.test.ts src/infra/outbound/echo.test.ts src/infra/outbound/echo-mirror-resolver.test.ts src/infra/outbound/echo-streaming.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-subpaths.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm tsgo:extensions
pnpm build:plugin-sdk:dts
```

Focused Marmorkrebs/Stryker mutation on the source-reply echo block in
`src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts` killed 14/19 mutants (73.68%). The surviving
mutants were truthy/optional-chain equivalents; the mutants that would regress delivery
options, filtering, payload, and role handling were killed.

### Native Telegram proof - webchat to Telegram

Mantis ran the native Telegram Desktop proof against the rendering head immediately
before the owner-binding SDK/API and lifecycle-guard changes:

- Run: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27172400676
- Mantis comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/88815#issuecomment-4654447721
- Artifact: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27172400676/artifacts/7494836149
- Baseline: `72f74b33e129233d11fce9d9676464ee009d0f46`, status `pass`
- Candidate: `e52925a313521f420f8ee8e0ac9cc6ab8c484a22`, status `pass`

The Mantis artifact includes before/after Telegram Desktop screenshots, GIFs, MP4 clips,
and JSON summaries. The candidate run started the gateway from that PR head, added a
Telegram echo target with `sessions.echo`, sent a webchat `chat.send` turn, and captured
the resulting Telegram Desktop delivery in the leased test group.

The current head's additional source delta is the registry ownership/lifecycle fix:
`plugin-sdk/channel-echo` no longer exports the registration function, Telegram registers
through the full plugin activation API, and `src/plugins/registry.runtime-config.test.ts`
proves the host rejects registration before channel ownership and stores the factory under
the owning plugin/channel id. `extensions/telegram/src/echo-renderer-register.test.ts`
also proves that a tool-discovery pass does not consume the later full registration
attempt.

### Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: A webchat `chat.send` turn pinned to a Telegram echo target should
  create a real Telegram-visible echo of the prompt and assistant response while avoiding
  duplicate prompt/final deliveries from the fallback hook paths. A plugin should not be
  able to claim another channel's native echo renderer registry slot.
- Real environment tested: Mantis Telegram Desktop proof workflow on GitHub Actions,
  using an OpenClaw crabbox, a leased test Telegram account/group, baseline
  `72f74b33e129233d11fce9d9676464ee009d0f46`, and candidate
  `e52925a313521f420f8ee8e0ac9cc6ab8c484a22`. Current-head source tests ran locally on
  `88943686569ebff43fecc25fc51be6b9bf621e3c` for the owner-binding registry and
  Telegram lifecycle-guard changes.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: The Mantis workflow resolved the PR head,
  checked out the baseline and candidate refs, started the candidate gateway, added a
  Telegram echo target through `sessions.echo` with `echoUser: true` and
  `echoAssistant: true`, sent `chat.send` with message `Mantis Telegram echo proof:
  mirror this webchat turn.`, then recorded Telegram Desktop screenshot/GIF/MP4 evidence.
  After the owner-binding and lifecycle-guard commits, focused current-head tests
  verified that public SDK registration is gone, Telegram uses the owner-scoped API, the
  registry binds the factory to the owning channel id, and tool-discovery cannot consume
  the later full Telegram renderer registration.
- Evidence after fix: Mantis published visual and metadata evidence for the delivery path:
  https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/88815#issuecomment-4654447721 and
  https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27172400676/artifacts/7494836149.
  Current-head tests additionally covered the SDK ownership and lifecycle fixes:
  `extensions/telegram/src/echo-renderer-register.test.ts`,
  `src/plugins/registry.runtime-config.test.ts`, and
  `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-subpaths.test.ts`.
- Observed result after fix: The Mantis run completed successfully and captured native
  Telegram Desktop proof for the webchat-to-Telegram echo path. Current-head tests confirm
  the public SDK no longer exposes owner-unbound registration and that the plugin registry
  binds renderer registration to the owning channel plugin. They also confirm a
  tool-discovery pass cannot mark Telegram echo registration complete before full
  activation owns the channel.
- What was not tested: Personal daily-use Telegram bot, non-Telegram streaming renderers,
  and final maintainer wording for operator-facing echo target docs.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The visible proof uses a leased Mantis
  Telegram test group with private details redacted by the artifact publisher. The
  screenshot/GIF proves the real Telegram delivery path; source-level tests cover the
  non-visible delivery gates, duplicate-suppression branches, and SDK owner-binding
  boundary.

### Review state

This branch addresses the code findings raised so far:

- source-reply final mirror bypassing the native-final / streaming gate
- duplicate direct `chat.send` prompt echo delivery
- public SDK registration surface allowing non-owning plugins to claim a channel
- Telegram tool-discovery lifecycle suppressing later full-mode echo renderer registration
- Telegram echo renderer failing to reuse configured Telegram client options

Remaining maintainer calls are product/API policy: operator-facing echo target docs,
privacy expectations for persistent cross-transport echo targets, and whether the new
owner-scoped plugin API should remain the stable renderer registration surface.

target.title: fix(models): keep generated secret refs out of plaintext
target.body:
Fixes #88562.

## Summary

- Persist generated env-backed SecretRef provider auth as `secretref-env:<NAME>` instead of a bare env var name.
- Resolve that marker through `process.env` at model registry request time.
- Treat string SecretRef markers as audit-safe while still flagging unresolved object SecretRefs and true plaintext strings.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Per-agent `models.json` generation could persist auth-profile env `keyRef` values as bare strings such as `TENSORIX_API_KEY`, which looked like plaintext to the deep security audit and produced false-positive `PLAINTEXT_FOUND` findings. The repair now covers both newly generated env-backed markers and stale existing `models.json` files when the current provider context has the matching env SecretRef.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/andy/openclaw-88562`, branch `fix/models-json-secret-ref-88562`, commit `06a7e81ddcc389d155544c405129963f1916f041`.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/secrets/audit.test.ts src/agents/models-config.applies-config-env-vars.test.ts src/agents/sessions/model-registry.test.ts
pnpm check:test-types
pnpm docs:list
git diff --check
pnpm exec oxfmt --check docs/gateway/config-tools.md src/secrets/audit.test.ts src/secrets/audit.ts
node --import tsx --input-type=module <models-json-secretref-current-head-proof>
git log --format='%h %an <%ae> %s' upstream/main..HEAD
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 25.98s
pnpm check:test-types: passed
pnpm docs:list: passed
git diff --check: passed
pnpm exec oxfmt --check docs/gateway/config-tools.md src/secrets/audit.test.ts src/secrets/audit.ts: passed

{
  "proof_head_sha": "06a7e81ddcc389d155544c405129963f1916f041",
  "generated_marker": {
    "apiKey": "secretref-env:TENSORIX_API_KEY",
    "equals_secretref_env_marker": true,
    "equals_bare_env_var": false,
    "runtime_resolved_matches_env": true,
    "models_json_plaintext_finding_count": 0
  },
  "stale_existing_marker": {
    "current_provider_secret_ref": "env:TENSORIX_API_KEY",
    "existing_models_apiKey": "TENSORIX_API_KEY",
    "audit_status": "clean",
    "models_json_plaintext_finding_count": 0,
    "models_json_findings": []
  },
  "mismatch_guardrail": {
    "existing_models_apiKey": "OTHER_TENSORIX_API_KEY",
    "models_json_plaintext_finding_count": 1,
    "models_json_findings": [
      {
        "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
        "jsonPath": "providers.tensorix.apiKey"
      }
    ]
  }
}

06a7e81ddc Andy Ye <35905412+TurboTheTurtle@users.noreply.github.com> Fix stale models json secret ref audit markers
e7741d176e Andy Ye <35905412+TurboTheTurtle@users.noreply.github.com> Fix models json secret ref api keys
```

Observed result after fix: A generated provider backed by an auth-profile env `keyRef` gets `apiKey: "secretref-env:TENSORIX_API_KEY"`, not the bare env var name. The model registry resolves that marker through `process.env.TENSORIX_API_KEY` at request time, and the security audit reports zero `PLAINTEXT_FOUND` findings for both the generated marker and a stale existing `models.json` value of `TENSORIX_API_KEY` when Tensorix is currently managed by that exact env SecretRef. A mismatched bare value, `OTHER_TENSORIX_API_KEY`, is still flagged as `PLAINTEXT_FOUND`, so arbitrary all-caps strings are not broadly whitelisted.

What was not tested: The source-tree CLI wrapper requires built `dist/entry.(m)js`, so the proof invoked the patched generator, model registry, and security audit runtime directly from source rather than `node openclaw.mjs security audit --json`.

## Notes

- Maintainer can modify: enabled.

target.title: fix: keep insecure Control UI auth disabled after onboarding
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #78855.

The QuickStart setup path could leave `gateway.controlUi.allowInsecureAuth=true` in generated setup config for loopback Control UI compatibility. This keeps the post-onboarding default safe by no longer enabling `allowInsecureAuth` unless the user or an existing config sets it explicitly.

## Security impact

`gateway.controlUi.allowInsecureAuth` is already treated as an insecure/dangerous flag by `openclaw security audit`. New QuickStart setup should not write that warning-class flag by default.

This does not remove the compatibility flag. Users who intentionally need it can still configure it explicitly, and explicit `allowInsecureAuth=true` remains supported and audited.

## Compatibility note

There is a legitimate compatibility reason this flag may have been generated historically: it preserves local Control UI usability for localhost token/password flows when the browser cannot provide device identity in an insecure HTTP context.

This PR does not claim that `allowInsecureAuth` is a full remote auth bypass. The runtime policy still treats it as a local-only compatibility toggle, while `dangerouslyDisableDeviceAuth` remains the break-glass bypass.

The security issue is narrower: QuickStart silently persists a warning-class security downgrade into generated config. A user can complete normal onboarding and end up with `gateway.controlUi.allowInsecureAuth=true` without making an explicit security choice, and `openclaw security audit` later reports that generated state as insecure.

This PR keeps the compatibility mechanism intact for users who intentionally need it. It only removes the automatic QuickStart writer path. If localhost token-only compatibility should remain part of onboarding, it should be made explicit with a setup prompt, warning, or documented opt-in path instead of silently persisting the insecure flag.

The current PR head also adds explicit regression coverage proving that QuickStart still preserves an intentional `gateway.controlUi.allowInsecureAuth=true` opt-in from existing config.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Interactive QuickStart/local onboarding should not leave `gateway.controlUi.allowInsecureAuth=true` in the generated config unless it was explicitly configured.
- Real environment tested: Local source checkout on Linux, Node via repo source runner, isolated `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` under `/home/rev/tmp/openclaw-78855-interactive-quickstart-source`; validated PR HEAD `01f2bc3e0d0fb5807dd28472509e50dcc66d76d0`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `script -qfec 'node -e "console.log(\"stdin isTTY:\", Boolean(process.stdin.isTTY)); console.log(\"stdout isTTY:\", Boolean(process.stdout.isTTY))"; node scripts/run-node.mjs onboard --flow quickstart --mode local --accept-risk --auth-choice skip --skip-channels --skip-skills --skip-search --skip-hooks --skip-daemon --skip-health --skip-ui' /dev/null`, then read `$OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` and checked `gateway.controlUi.allowInsecureAuth`.
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from `/tmp/openclaw-pr78855-interactive-source-proof.log`:

```text
HEAD: 01f2bc3e0d0fb5807dd28472509e50dcc66d76d0
stdin isTTY: true
stdout isTTY: true
OpenClaw 2026.5.31 (01f2bc3)

OpenClaw setup
QuickStart
  Gateway port: 18789
  Gateway bind: Loopback (127.0.0.1)
  Gateway auth: Token (default)
  Tailscale exposure: Off
  Direct to chat channels.

Updated config: /home/rev/tmp/openclaw-78855-interactive-quickstart-source/config/openclaw.json
Workspace OK: $OPENCLAW_HOME/.openclaw/workspace
Sessions OK: /home/rev/tmp/openclaw-78855-interactive-quickstart-source/state/agents/main/sessions

Control UI
  Web UI: http://127.0.0.1:18789/
  Web UI (with token): <redacted tokenized local URL>
  Gateway WS: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
  Gateway: not detected (unauthorized: gateway token mismatch (provide gateway auth token))
  Docs: https://docs.openclaw.ai/web/control-ui

Onboarding complete. Use the dashboard link above to control OpenClaw.

CONFIG CHECK:
config: /home/rev/tmp/openclaw-78855-interactive-quickstart-source/config/openclaw.json
{}
OK: allowInsecureAuth is not true
```

- Observed result after fix: A TTY-backed interactive QuickStart/local setup through `runSetupWizard` and `configureGatewayForSetup` completed in the isolated config, and the final `gateway.controlUi` object was `{}`; `gateway.controlUi.allowInsecureAuth` was not `true`.
- What was not tested: Full daemon install and browser launch were not run; this PR is scoped to the shared QuickStart setup writer and generated config state. The Control UI summary path was exercised with `--skip-ui`, and the expected local Gateway probe reported not detected because no daemon was started in the isolated proof run.

## Additional proof details

Before patch, detached `origin/main` at `49e5091f180e3c1fd35d39b449c074d0f8670f60` still showed the QuickStart setup writer enabling the flag:

```text
COMMAND: node --import tsx --input-type=module <inline configureGatewayForSetup quickstart loopback>
{
  "allowInsecureAuth": true
}
VULNERABLE: allowInsecureAuth remains true after QuickStart setup writer
```

After patch, setup writer proof at validated head `01f2bc3e0d0fb5807dd28472509e50dcc66d76d0`:

```text
COMMAND: node --import tsx --input-type=module <inline configureGatewayForSetup quickstart loopback>
{}
OK: allowInsecureAuth is not true
```

Current PR head `04eac2dc034990a6c3862ca491518732d386b13d` adds test-only coverage for the explicit compatibility path:

```text
preserves explicit insecure local control ui auth opt-in in quickstart
```

Security audit still flags explicit insecure config:

```text
gateway.control_ui.insecure_auth Control UI insecure auth toggle enabled
config.insecure_or_dangerous_flags Insecure or dangerous config flag enabled
  Detected enabled flag: gateway.controlUi.allowInsecureAuth=true.
```

Safe generated config does not trigger the insecure-auth warning:

```text
{}
OK: safe onboarding audit did not report allowInsecureAuth
```

Note: local `openclaw security audit` printed an unrelated source-checkout warning about the WhatsApp persistedAuthState checker missing `@openclaw/normalization-core/string-coerce`; the audit still completed and produced the expected insecure-auth findings.

## Validation

Validation log: `/tmp/openclaw-pr78855-validation.log`
Proof logs: `/tmp/openclaw-pr78855-proof.log`, `/tmp/openclaw-pr78855-interactive-source-proof.log`

Validated head `01f2bc3e0d0fb5807dd28472509e50dcc66d76d0`:

- `env OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.wizard.config.ts src/wizard/setup.gateway-config.test.ts` - 1 file, 11 tests passed
- `env OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server/ws-connection/connect-policy.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.test.ts` - 2 files, 38 tests passed
- `env OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/security/audit-gateway-exposure.test.ts` - 1 file, 15 tests passed
- `env OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-startup-log.test.ts` - 1 file, 8 tests passed
- `pnpm -s exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 --config .oxfmtrc.jsonc src/wizard/setup.gateway-config.ts src/wizard/setup.gateway-config.test.ts` - passed
- `git diff --check` - passed
- `timeout 240s pnpm -s tsgo:core` - passed

Follow-up commit `04eac2dc034990a6c3862ca491518732d386b13d` is test-only and adds explicit opt-in preservation coverage. The targeted wizard command above should now report 12 tests when rerun at current head.

Not run: full `pnpm test` and full `pnpm lint`; this PR is intentionally scoped to the QuickStart setup writer and nearby gateway/security coverage above.

target.title: docs: document cli command path helpers
target.body:
## Summary
- Documents exported CLI command-path matching helpers.
- Documents CLI JSON output helpers for pre-parse flag detection and stderr routing restoration.
- Documents CLI respawn policy helpers and the foreground gateway no-respawn invariant.
- Documents CLI prompt helpers for stdin close handling and global `--yes` behavior.
- Documents CLI completion runtime helpers for shell detection, cache/profile paths, and profile command formatting.
- Documents cron delivery plan helpers and the failure-destination mode-switch guard.
- Documents cron session target helpers for persistent/current targets, delivery routing, and failure notification fallback sessions.
- Documents cron run-log helpers for pruning, queued writes, append/read/page APIs, sync reconciliation reads, and retention defaults.
- Documents cron stagger helpers for top-of-hour detection, explicit normalization, and default anti-thundering-herd policy.
- Documents cron schedule identity helpers for timer reconciliation.
- Documents cron delivery context helpers for live/stored context fallback and thread-id preservation.
- Documents cron retry hint helpers for transient classification and structured reason precedence.
- Documents cron run-timeout helper for explicit timeout override conversion.
- Documents cron utility helpers for active-job state, identity normalization, absolute-time parsing, and delivery field parsing.
- Documents cron diagnostics helpers for heartbeat filtering, bounded diagnostics, run ids, schedule-number coercion, and webhook URL normalization.
- Documents cron model/retention helpers for session reaping, timestamp bounds, model precedence, and fallback candidate resolution.
- Documents cron service policy helpers for manual wake routing, timeout/error text policy, and failure alert cooldown behavior.
- Documents cron service runtime helpers for normalization, mutable state construction, task-ledger records, and watchdog cleanup.
- Documents cron service store helpers for initial delivery defaults, store locking, quarantine, and persistence.
- Documents cron service job helpers for backoff, schedule recompute, creation/patching, due checks, and payload extraction.
- Documents cron service ops helpers for lifecycle, read, mutation, manual run, queued run, and wake APIs.
- Documents cron service timer helpers for timeout wrapping, result application, timer ticks, startup catch-up, execution, and event dispatch.
- Documents cron normalization helpers for input/create/patch normalization, canonical schedule field pruning, session-target safety, and isolated-agent default-delivery semantics.
- Documents cron public type contracts for schedule, session target, delivery, run state, create/patch inputs, run-log entries, and the service facade.
- Documents cron service state contracts for lifecycle events, logger/dependency injection, mutable state, run results, and operation serialization.
- Documents cron list-page contracts for enabled/schedule/status filters, sort options, request options, and offset-page results.
- Documents cron persisted parsing contracts for legacy run-log JSONL rows and structural rejection reasons.
- Documents cron task-ledger contracts for active-run progress text and explicit session drill-down precedence.
- Documents cron store type contracts for quarantine sidecars, config-job runtime metadata, loaded stores, and SQLite row shapes.
- Documents cron job scheduling helpers for default error backoff, schedulable timestamps, and webhook/channel destination clearing.
- Documents cron delivery target contracts for announcement/failure-notification routing and timeout-bounded best-effort failure sends.
- Documents cron isolated-agent delivery target resolution contracts and stale-thread carry-over guard.
- Documents cron isolated-agent payload outcome contracts and fatal structured-error precedence.
- Documents cron isolated-agent model-selection and local-provider preflight result contracts.
- Documents cron isolated-agent mutable session-state contracts.
- Documents cron isolated-agent delivery dispatch and final turn-result contracts.
- Documents cron public telemetry, diagnostics, delivery trace, failure-alert, and patch contracts.
- Documents cron service add/update/list input and result alias contracts.
- Documents cron isolated-agent run entrypoint and top-of-hour stagger default contracts.
- Documents outbound reply-to policy contracts and single-use implicit reply consumption.
- Documents session-bound delivery router contracts and requesterless ambiguity fallback.
- Documents outbound thread-id normalization and delivery commit hook contracts.
- Documents outbound target provider/kind/topic prefix helpers and cross-channel validation.
- Documents outbound session context builder and agent-id derivation policy.
- Documents outbound transcript mirror payloads and delivery-layer group/channel correlation metadata.
- Documents outbound directory cache key, TTL, capacity, and config-reference invalidation contracts.
- Documents outbound message-tool allowlist, cross-context policy, marker decoration, and rich-presentation contracts.
- Documents outbound target normalization, plugin target resolution, id-like heuristics, and resolver signature contracts.
- Documents outbound send dependency legacy-key derivation and channel send dependency lookup contracts.
- Documents outbound message action target modes and target-presence detection contracts.
- Documents outbound session-binding target/status/placement/error, conversation ref, binding record, bind/unbind input, and capability contracts.
- Documents outbound message planning overrides, text/media units, chunking, reply-to consumption, and media caption contracts.
- Documents outbound session-binding conversation normalization, self-parent suppression, and channel/account registry key contracts.
- Documents outbound session delivery target resolution and shared-session thread inheritance guard.
- Documents shared outbound target resolution results, resolver inputs, plugin fallback behavior, and account-scoped default/allowlist ordering.
- Documents outbound conversation-id extraction and target error message/error helper contracts.
- Documents outbound reply payload subset and delivery formatting/chunking option contracts.
- Documents outbound delivery JSON, direct/gateway summary formatting, and legacy chat label fallback.
- Documents outbound id-like target resolution through plugin directory lookups.
- Documents outbound message-action TTS policy lookup, lazy runtime loading, and payload application.
- Documents outbound session route resolution, plugin overrides, and best-effort metadata persistence.
- Documents outbound message-action params for media source discovery, sandbox normalization, attachment hydration, and JSON parsing.
- Documents outbound gateway option defaults and managed-endpoint URL suppression.
- Documents outbound best-effort delivery decisions and result envelope flattening.
- Documents outbound channel resolution runtime facades, channel normalization, plugin resolution, and read-only metadata lookup.
- Documents outbound identity metadata normalization and agent identity resolution.
- Documents the outbound conversation-id guard for provider-native colon targets.
- Documents source-delivery target prefix comparison across case-sensitive and case-normalized providers.
- Documents account-scoped conversation binding manager and session-binding adapter registration contracts.
- Documents the bound-delivery router factory for active session-binding resolution.
- Documents outbound message channel selection ids, sources, and config resolution contracts.
- Documents outbound channel bootstrap and retry-guard reset contracts.
- Documents public message channel durability, receipt, render-plan, send lifecycle, live, receive, adapter, and durable intent contracts.
- Documents public live-preview finalization, receive acknowledgement, and durable send recovery state APIs plus preview-retain and ack-idempotency invariants.
- Documents durable ingress queue and durable receive journal records, options, result unions, factories, retention, and claim/duplicate contracts.
- Documents channel turn admission, event/access facts, delivery adapters/results, record/history options, resolved/prepared turn shapes, log events, adapters, and run params.
- Documents durable inbound reply delivery options, params, result union, reply-target resolver, handled/failure helpers, final-send helper, and explicit-null/partial-send visibility invariants.
- Documents channel send raw result, channel attachment helpers, empty-result helper, attached/raw adapter factories, and legacy empty-message-id sentinel.
- Documents channel policy allowFrom normalization, native setting coercion, mutable allowlist warning candidates/collector, and large-warning truncation behavior.
- Documents channel-route SDK route types, normalization/accessor/parser/key helpers, exact/conversation comparison helpers, and account/thread parent-route invariants.
- Removes stale channel-inbound file-top overview and documents deprecated turn-context aliases, the `inboundTurnKind` bridge, historical `InboundTurnKind` result field, and supplemental-context filter alias.
- Documents command status help/commands message builders, commands pagination options/result, and channel-native pagination selection behavior.
- Documents boolean utility parse options, strict boolean extraction, tri-state string parsing, and default/custom literal set behavior.
- Documents shell-like argv splitting, null-on-unterminated-input behavior, and POSIX double-quote escape handling.
- Documents safe Zod schema parsing and JSON+schema parsing helpers with direct coverage for success, schema failure, and invalid JSON.
- Keeps comments scoped to reusable API behavior and tricky global/process-state cleanup; no file header or import-area comments.

## Verification

list-page contracts for filters, sort options, request options, and offset-page results, cron delivery target contracts for announcement/failure-notification routing and timeout-bounded best-effort failure sends, and cron run-log retention default contracts, cron isolated-agent delivery target resolution contracts plus the stale-thread carry-over guard, and cron isolated-agent payload outcome contracts plus fatal structured-error precedence, cron isolated-agent model-selection/local-provider preflight contracts, and cron isolated-agent mutable session-state contracts, plus outbound target.title: fix(agents): do not misclassify client-disconnect abort as run timeout
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #90764 reports that embedded agent runs abort at ~165–180 s wall-clock regardless of `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` or `models.providers.*.timeoutSeconds`. The run exits with "Request timed out before a response was generated." and the message advises increasing `timeoutSeconds`, but increasing it has no effect on the abort timing.
- **Root Cause**: `onAbort` and `onExternalAbortSignal` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` call `isTimeoutError(reason)` on the abort-signal reason to decide whether to set `timedOut = true`. `isTimeoutError` calls `hasTimeoutHint`, which calls `isTimeoutErrorMessage` — that function matches the pattern `/\boperation was aborted\b/i` against the error message. `AbortController.abort()` with no arguments produces `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")`; that message matches the pattern, so `hasTimeoutHint` returns `true` and `isTimeoutError` returns `true` for what is actually a plain client cancellation, setting `timedOut = true` erroneously. `watchClientDisconnect` in `src/gateway/http-common.ts` calls `abortController.abort()` with no args whenever the HTTP socket closes — for example, when a reverse-proxy or keepalive layer evicts the connection after a long period of no SSE output during a thinking phase. This fires `params.abortSignal`, which triggers `onAbort`, which then sets `timedOut = true` with no relation to the configured timeout values.
- **Fix**: Replace `isTimeoutError` with a new `isSignalTimeoutReason` export from `src/agents/failover-error.ts`. `isSignalTimeoutReason` checks only `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` — the name produced exclusively by `AbortSignal.timeout()` and `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`, the two intentional timeout-creation paths. Plain `AbortError` (`name = "AbortError"`) is a cancellation and returns `false`. `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all other callers that classify general LLM error messages, where the Ollama NDJSON / stream-abort patterns still apply.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/failover-error.ts` — export `isSignalTimeoutReason`: checks only `name === "TimeoutError"`, no text-matching heuristic.
  - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` — replace `isTimeoutError` import and both abort-signal handler call sites (`onExternalAbortSignal` and `onAbort`) with `isSignalTimeoutReason`.
  - `src/agents/failover-error.test.ts` — add `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe block: plain `AbortError` DOMException, ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE `AbortError`, `AbortSignal.timeout()` DOMException, `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`-style Error, null/undefined.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - `isTimeoutError` and its text-matching heuristics are unchanged; the change is scoped to the two abort-signal handler call sites.
  - Config surface unchanged (no schema, defaults, doctor migrations, or `docs/reference/config`).
  - Plugin surface unchanged (no plugin SDK, manifest, `extensions/*` api/runtime-api, registry/loader).
  - Gateway protocol unchanged.

### Reproduction

1. Configure `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds: 600` and `models.providers.custom-api-anthropic-com.timeoutSeconds: 600`.
2. Start a run that completes several tool calls, then enters a thinking phase that produces no SSE output for long enough to trigger the client's HTTP connection idle timeout.
3. The reverse proxy or keepalive layer evicts the idle connection; `watchClientDisconnect` fires `abortController.abort()` with no args.
4. **Before this PR**: `onAbort` calls `isTimeoutError(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` — `hasTimeoutHint` matches the message via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` (`/\boperation was aborted\b/i`), returns `true`; `timedOut = true`; run exits at ~165–180 s with "Request timed out before a response was generated."; increasing `timeoutSeconds` has no effect.
5. **After this PR**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` checks `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` → `false`; `timedOut` is not set; the abort is treated as an external cancellation, not a run timeout.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#90764)**: after the fix, a client-disconnect abort no longer sets `timedOut = true` in the embedded attempt; only abort-signal reasons with `name === "TimeoutError"` (from `AbortSignal.timeout()` or `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`) are treated as run timeouts.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 22 — Vitest against the production `isSignalTimeoutReason` and the two production abort-signal handler paths)**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` driven directly with each discriminating input; `onAbort` / `onExternalAbortSignal` call sites exercised via the existing full-runner `attempt.test.ts` suite.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts`; `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`; `pnpm format:check` and `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the three changed files; `pnpm tsgo`.

**Evidence after fix**:

```
failover-error.test.ts   Tests  88 passed (88)
attempt.test.ts          Tests  121 passed (121)
```

The `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe covers: `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")` → `false` (watchClientDisconnect regression case; old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` matching `/\boperation was aborted\b/i`); `Error("request aborted", name="AbortError")` → `false` (old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE`); `DOMException("signal timed out", "TimeoutError")` → `true`; `Error("request timed out", name="TimeoutError")` → `true`; null/undefined → `false`.

**Observed result after fix**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(new DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` returns `false`; the abort-signal handlers classify client disconnections as `externalAbort=true / timedOut=false`; the run does not surface "Request timed out" for network-layer connection evictions.

**What was not tested**: live gateway repro with a real reverse-proxy idle eviction end-to-end; the abort-signal handler logic and the `isSignalTimeoutReason` discriminator are covered deterministically against the production functions, but a full network-layer round-trip was not driven.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` is strictly narrower than `isTimeoutError` for the two affected call sites; a timeout path that passes `params.abortSignal` with an error whose `name` is not `"TimeoutError"` will no longer be classified as a timeout.
- **Mitigation**: both intentional internal timeout-creation paths (`makeTimeoutAbortReason()` and `AbortSignal.timeout()`) produce `name === "TimeoutError"`; `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all LLM error classification callers where the broader text-matching heuristic is appropriate.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Agent runtime
- [x] Tests

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #90764

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches

target.title: [AI-assisted] Persist agent-run error replies in chat history
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A started Control UI agent run could surface a terminal provider/runtime error live, but `chat.history` after refresh did not retain an assistant error turn for that failed run.
- Solution: Treat agent-run failure replies as real error payloads and make Gateway persist exactly one display-safe assistant error transcript entry for returned agent errors, even when the runtime emitted only an internal error stub.
- What changed: Runner failure payloads are marked `isError`, Gateway appends a deduped `<runId>:assistant-error` transcript entry for returned agent errors, and regression coverage now locks the returned-error, runtime-stub, and duplicate/idempotency paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No provider behavior changes, no delivery routing changes, no config/lifecycle changes, no raw transcript writes, and no dependency or lockfile changes.

## Motivation

- A user should not lose the visible assistant error after refreshing the Control UI. If a started run fails before normal assistant output, the live error event and durable history should agree.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #89197
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A real Gateway/WebSocket Control UI run that fails at provider transport now leaves exactly one assistant error in refreshed `chat.history`.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout with the patched PR branch, real Gateway server startup, real WebSocket RPC client, channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, and an intentionally unreachable OpenAI-compatible base URL to trigger the provider failure without secrets.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- -t 'assistant error|returned agent-run error payloads|runtime emitted an internal error stub'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.error-broadcast.test.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -- -t 'Agent failed before reply|assistant error|fallback candidate|error stub'
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node --import tsx ../real-chat-history-proof.ts after-gateway-force-v3
/usr/bin/time -v node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Real runtime proof:
- provider transport failed with fetch failed / Connection error
- agent.wait status: error
- immediateHistoryCount: 3
- refreshHistoryCount: 3
- immediateAssistantCount: 1
- refreshAssistantCount: 1
- chatErrorEventCount: 1
- result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY

Focused Gateway proof: 3 passed, 102 skipped.
Adjacent Gateway proof: 107 passed.
Session tool result guard proof: 35 passed.
Runner/Followup focused proof: 8 passed, 214 skipped.
git diff --check: clean.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live provider failure still broadcasts the expected chat error, and both immediate and post-reconnect `chat.history` contain one assistant error text: `Agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: network connection error.`
- What was not tested: Full repository-wide test suite, successful `tsgo` core on this testserver, real provider credentials, and a browser screenshot.
- Before evidence:

```text
Unpatched main proof for the same real Gateway/WebSocket/provider-failure path:
- sendStatus: started
- agent.wait status: ok
- immediateHistoryCount: 2
- refreshHistoryCount: 2
- immediateAssistantCount: 0
- refreshAssistantCount: 0
- chatErrorEventCount: 0
- result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Runner final failure replies were not consistently marked as error payloads, and Gateway treated the runtime assistant-error callback as if it guaranteed a Control UI-visible `chat.history` assistant turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Previous coverage asserted live error broadcast behavior but did not prove refreshed `chat.history` retained an assistant error after a started real runtime failure.
- Contributing context (if known): Runtime/session guard error stubs can be internal transcript-only markers; Gateway still needs to durably mirror returned terminal agent errors into the Control UI history path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`, and the temporary real Gateway/WebSocket proof harness.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A started run returns an agent error; Gateway appends one assistant error transcript entry, keeps the live error terminal event, survives reconnect/history refresh, and does not duplicate the assistant error.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The source tests cover the exact Gateway and runner seams, while the real proof exercises server startup, WebSocket RPC, provider transport failure, `agent.wait`, and refreshed `chat.history` in one flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing broadcast tests covered visible error delivery but not durable assistant transcript history.
- If no new test is added, why not: New regression assertions were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Control UI users should now see the same failed assistant turn after refresh that they saw live when a started agent run fails before normal assistant output.

## Diagram

```text
real provider/runtime failure
  -> runner returns final failure payload marked isError
  -> Gateway returnedAgentErrorPayloads branch
  -> append one sanitized assistant error transcript entry with <runId>:assistant-error
  -> broadcast existing chat error terminal event
  -> reconnect chat.history still includes one assistant error
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No new scope; the change writes a sanitized assistant error to the same session transcript path already used for Gateway assistant persistence.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test checkout
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw repository scripts with installed dependencies
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible provider configured to an unreachable local base URL for deterministic transport failure; no real credentials used
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI-style Gateway WebSocket RPC; external channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`
- Relevant config (redacted): Dummy redacted API key, local unreachable base URL, temporary state directory

### Steps

1. Start the real Gateway server from the patched checkout.
2. Connect a WebSocket client and send `chat.send` for a Control UI session.
3. Trigger a deterministic provider transport failure through the unreachable model base URL.
4. Wait for the run with `agent.wait`.
5. Read `chat.history` immediately.
6. Reconnect a fresh WebSocket client and read `chat.history` again.
7. Count assistant text messages and chat error events.

### Expected

- The run surfaces a live terminal error.
- Refreshed `chat.history` contains exactly one assistant error message for the failed run.
- The assistant error is not duplicated.

### Actual

- Before: refreshed `chat.history` had zero assistant messages for the failed run.
- After: refreshed `chat.history` had exactly one assistant error message and one chat error event.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Before real proof result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
After real proof result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY
After source proof: Gateway focused 3/3, Gateway adjacent 107/107, session guard 35/35, Runner/Followup focused 8/8.
TypeScript core check on the testserver did not produce diagnostics; the process was terminated by signal 9 at about 1.78 GB RSS, so this is reported as a testserver resource blocker rather than a green typecheck claim.
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Returned agent-run error payload detection, runner final failure `isError` marking, Gateway assistant-error transcript append, runtime internal error-stub callback interaction, idempotency key behavior, real provider transport failure, immediate history, refreshed history after reconnect, and existing chat error event behavior.
- Edge cases checked: Runtime internal error stub does not suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript; Gateway writes one `<runId>:assistant-error` entry for the returned terminal error; no dependency or lockfile files changed.
- What you did **not** verify: Full repo-wide suite, successful `tsgo` core on the resource-limited testserver, real external provider credentials, screenshot/recording, and production channels.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Not applicable.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A runtime-internal error stub could suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript.
  - Mitigation: Returned terminal agent errors now force the Gateway assistant-error transcript append while retaining the existing idempotency key.
- Risk: Gateway could duplicate assistant errors.
  - Mitigation: The transcript append uses a stable `<runId>:assistant-error` idempotency key and the source tests cover the one-message behavior.
- Risk: Error text could bypass display sanitization.
  - Mitigation: The Gateway fallback still uses `sanitizeAssistantDisplayText` before transcript append.
- Risk: Dependency Guard could require security approval.
  - Mitigation: No dependency manifests, lockfiles, or dependency blocks were changed.

target.title: fix(cli): avoid false local gateway unreachable on probe timeout
target.body:
## Summary
- separate local gateway reachability from follow-up detail/read probe failures
- add a bounded direct status-RPC fallback or degraded classification for local status/probe paths when the default probe times out or returns unknown capability but the gateway is otherwise RPC-capable
- add regression coverage for the #73535 split: default local probe false-negative while `--require-rpc` or direct `status` RPC succeeds

## Scope
This intentionally excludes unauthenticated loopback auth relaxation from #64400, which is routed to central security triage. The fix should stay in the ordinary status/probe classification and fallback path.

## Credit
Fixes the non-security path tracked in #48360. Uses reproduction evidence from #62762 and #73535, with historical evidence from #51357 and #42019. Thanks @RacecarGuy, @justinschille, @DJBlackhawk, @tianyaqpzm, and @0xrsydn.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- What was not tested: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451
